profile.name: Terry P.
profile.quote: Something feels off, but I keep telling myself it’s probably nothing serious.
profile.bio: Terry spent 35 years solving problems with precision — and he's approached his prostate cancer diagnosis the same way. Diagnosed with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer at 62 after a rising PSA prompted his annual physical, he has navigated eight years of escalating treatment with methodical determination. Terry reads every clinical summary his oncologist shares, keeps a spreadsheet tracking his PSA over time, and rarely misses an appointment. His wife Linda has been at every visit since the metastatic diagnosis.
profile.bio_1: Terry's cancer journey began with what felt like a manageable problem — a localized Gleason 7 tumor, a clear surgical plan, and a team that projected confidence. For two years post-prostatectomy, he believed it was behind him. When his PSA began climbing again at 18 months, he pushed his urologist for answers faster than most patients would. That instinct — questioning, tracking, advocating — has defined every step since.
profile.bio_2: By the time Terry reached his fourth treatment line, he had become fluent in the language of mCRPC in a way few patients are. He researches his options before each oncology visit, cross-references his labs against published trial data, and brings written questions. He doesn't always like the answers, but he needs to hear them clearly. His enrollment in ABO-701 was not a leap of faith — it was a calculated decision made after weeks of independent review.
profile.goal1: Extend meaningful, functional life — not just PSA suppression
profile.goal2: Understand every treatment decision before consenting to it
profile.goal3: Protect Linda from bearing the full weight of care coordination alone
profile.barrier1: Cumulative financial toxicity — lifetime out-of-pocket costs now exceed $140,000
profile.barrier2: Geographic burden of authorized treatment centers for Lu-177 and the trial site
profile.barrier3: Fatigue and cognitive dulling from long-term ADT limit the focus he relies on most

journey.narrative_description: Prostatectomy is completed with curative intent. Terry enters this phase optimistic and recovery-focused — a clear treatment plan with a defined endpoint provides psychological structure. Side effects are managed and the oncology team communicates confidence.
journey.quote: We caught it. The plan is clear, and I'm going to get through this.
sentiment_reasons: Curative intent framing provides strong psychological grounding
Local treatment at a familiar community hospital reduces logistical anxiety
Active treatment feels like progress — terry is 'doing something'

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Optimism about curative potential supports a moderately positive emotional state, even amid surgery or radiation side effects.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The urologist and radiation oncologist are experienced and communicative; terry feels guided and supported.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry defers heavily to clinical judgment at this stage and has limited independent knowledge of disease biology.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Treatment is local and time-bounded; out-of-pocket costs are significant but manageable with insurance.

events.label: Curative-intent surgery or radiation completed
events.description: Prostatectomy or external beam radiation delivered with curative intent. Patient completes a defined course and begins recovery.

events.label: Oncology team briefs patient on recovery expectations
events.description: Medical team provides structured post-treatment guidance, reinforcing the curative framing and setting PSA monitoring expectations.

journey.narrative_description: PSA reaches nadir and stabilizes. Terry resumes near-normal daily life and shifts to routine monitoring every 3–6 months. This period is experienced as validation — treatment worked. Life recedes toward normalcy and the disease moves to the background.
journey.quote: Numbers look great. I feel like myself again.
sentiment_reasons: PSA nadir is interpreted as treatment success — a powerful emotional signal
Return to normal routines reduces the salience of illness identity
Infrequent, low-burden monitoring requires minimal logistical effort

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Stable PSA and resumed normal life produce the highest emotional positivity in the journey.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Confidence in the oncology team is reinforced by the treatment appearing to have worked.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry remains in a provider-dependent orientation; there is no disease pressure to self-educate further.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Monitoring visits are local, routine, and covered under standard insurance with low out-of-pocket burden.

events.label: PSA nadir confirmed — no evidence of metastatic disease
events.description: PSA drops to its lowest point following primary treatment and remains stable. Clinical team confirms no evidence of metastatic spread.

journey.narrative_description: PSA begins rising approximately 18–24 months after primary treatment. Salvage radiation is attempted. Terry experiences anxiety but remains reassured by the oncology team, who frame this as a manageable recurrence with active treatment options still available.
journey.quote: The PSA is creeping up. They say there's still a plan — I'm trying to trust that.
sentiment_reasons: PSA rise after a period of stability is psychologically destabilizing
Provider reassurance partially offsets anxiety, but the curative narrative is compromised
First referral to an academic center introduces logistical friction and travel burden

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Anxiety and uncertainty temper terry's mood, but provider reassurance prevents a sharp emotional drop.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The oncology team's responsiveness with a salvage plan preserves trust despite the recurrence.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry begins to ask more questions but still relies heavily on clinical guidance for decision-making.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Salvage radiation may require travel to an academic center for the first time; cumulative out-of-pocket costs begin rising.

events.label: PSA rising — biochemical recurrence confirmed
events.description: PSA rises above 0.2 ng/mL following primary therapy. The clinical team initiates workup and considers salvage radiation.

events.label: Salvage radiation initiated
events.description: Second course of radiation targeting the prostate bed or pelvic nodes is begun. Patient is oriented toward treatment completion.

journey.narrative_description: Metastatic disease is confirmed on bone scan and CT. The psychological shift from 'curable' to 'manageable' is profound. ADT is initiated and terry begins confronting a fundamentally altered disease trajectory. The oncology nurse navigator is introduced as a new coordination resource.
journey.quote: Manageable — that's what they're calling it now. I'm trying to understand what that means for my life.
sentiment_reasons: The shift from curative to palliative framing is a major psychological rupture
Bone and lymph node metastases make the systemic nature of disease viscerally real
ADT side effects begin immediately — hot flashes, fatigue, and libido loss alter daily identity

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Confirmation of metastatic disease and the loss of curative hope produces a significant emotional drop.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The medical oncologist assumes the primary role; terry begins building trust with a new key clinician.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry has no framework for understanding metastatic PCa management and feels dependent on the team.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Lupron injections require ongoing office visits and prior authorization; copay assistance programs add administrative burden.

events.label: Bone scan and CT confirm metastatic disease
events.description: Imaging reveals bone and lymph node metastases. Patient is reclassified as mHSPC. The curative narrative is formally retired.

events.label: ADT initiated — Lupron depot injections begun
events.description: LHRH agonist therapy begins. Copay assistance program enrollment initiated. Oncology nurse navigator introduced.

events.label: Copay assistance program enrollment
events.description: Patient is enrolled in manufacturer copay assistance to manage Lupron injection costs. Navigator coordinates enrollment.

journey.narrative_description: ADT is combined with a first-line ARPI (enzalutamide). PSA drops significantly and disease stabilizes over 12–18 months. Terry experiences a 'second wind' — a period of treatment-driven optimism. Oral at-home therapy avoids infusion center visits, reducing logistical burden.
journey.quote: The numbers are dropping fast. I know it won't last forever, but right now this is working.
sentiment_reasons: Rapid PSA decline on enzalutamide produces strong, evidence-driven optimism
Oral at-home administration avoids infusion center burden and preserves routine
Disease stability on treatment reactivates the 'managing it' psychological frame

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Treatment response creates a genuine period of cautious optimism — the best emotional state since PSA nadir.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The medical oncologist's treatment choice is validated by measurable PSA response.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry learns basic concepts of CRPC and ARPI mechanisms but remains dependent on clinical guidance.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Specialty mail-order pharmacy for enzalutamide adds a new coordination touchpoint but removes infusion visits entirely.

events.label: PSA declines sharply on enzalutamide + ADT
events.description: PSA falls below 1 ng/mL within months of ARPI initiation. Restaging imaging shows stable bone disease.

events.label: Patient researches enzalutamide online
events.description: Patient discovers trial data and patient forums discussing enzalutamide. Anxiety about eventual resistance begins to surface.

journey.narrative_description: The cumulative side effect burden of ADT and enzalutamide becomes increasingly intrusive — fatigue, hot flashes, cognitive dulling, muscle loss, and loss of libido alter daily identity. Terry continues to show measurable disease response but quality of life deteriorates quietly. Ancillary medications multiply.
journey.quote: The cancer is quiet but I'm not. I don't feel like myself anymore.
sentiment_reasons: Persistent side effects erode quality of life even while PSA remains controlled
Loss of libido and cognitive changes directly challenge masculine identity
Growing pharmacy complexity adds low-level logistical friction that accumulates

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Side effects are experienced as ongoing losses — energy, sexuality, cognitive sharpness — producing a slow emotional drain.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The oncology team responds to side effect reports but solutions are limited; trust is maintained but expectations lower.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry becomes more proactive about side effect management but is not empowered to change the treatment trajectory.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Ancillary medications add ~$100–300/month in cost and require coordination across PCP, cardiologist, and oncologist.

events.label: ADT-related metabolic syndrome emerging
events.description: Hot flashes, hypertension, and dyslipidemia prompt referral to cardiologist and consideration of endocrinology consult.

events.label: Bone density monitoring — DEXA scan ordered
events.description: Oncologist orders DEXA scan to assess bone density loss from long-term ADT. Prior authorization required.

journey.narrative_description: PSA rises despite castrate testosterone levels — castration resistance is confirmed. Terry crosses a significant threshold. Docetaxel chemotherapy is initiated over 6–9 months. Infusion center visits add new logistical burden. Hair loss, neutropenia risk, and neuropathy emerge as new concerns.
journey.quote: I thought the pill was working. Now they're talking about chemo — I didn't think we'd be here this soon.
sentiment_reasons: Confirmation of castration resistance reframes the prognosis in a more threatening direction
Transition to intravenous chemotherapy marks a visible escalation that patients and families find alarming
Infusion center visits every 3 weeks restructure daily life and require caregiver coordination

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The mCRPC diagnosis and chemotherapy initiation produce genuine fear — the disease is no longer responding to the prior 'steady' plan.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The oncology team is proactive and communicative about the escalation, which sustains trust despite the setback.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry begins to understand the sequential nature of mCRPC treatment lines, increasing awareness if not control.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Infusion visits every 3 weeks, port placement, and Xgeva administration add significant scheduling and travel burden.

events.label: Castration resistance confirmed — PSA rising on ADT
events.description: PSA rises above 150 ng/mL despite castrate-level testosterone. New bone lesions visible on imaging. Disease reclassified as mCRPC.

events.label: Docetaxel + Xgeva regimen initiated
events.description: Chemotherapy regimen begins at the infusion center. Social worker engaged for financial assistance navigation.

events.label: Social worker engagement for financial assistance
events.description: Oncology social worker activated to coordinate financial assistance for Xgeva and docetaxel costs approaching out-of-pocket maximum.

journey.narrative_description: Post-chemotherapy restaging shows mixed response. A second-line ARPI (abiraterone + prednisone) is initiated. Terry is fatigued but disease is temporarily controlled. First exposure to an academic oncology team through second opinion opens awareness of clinical trial options.
journey.quote: Back to the pills — I'll take it. But I can feel we're running out of road.
sentiment_reasons: Return to oral therapy feels like a reprieve after chemotherapy's intensity
Mixed imaging response creates ambiguity — partial success that doesn't fully reassure
First trial awareness at academic center introduces both hope and the realization that options may be narrowing

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Terry is exhausted and aware of disease progression, but temporary control and return to oral therapy prevent a sharp emotional drop.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Exposure to an NCI-designated academic oncology team expands terry's trust network and introduces new clinical voices.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Patient begins actively researching treatment lines and asking more targeted questions about what comes after abiraterone.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Oral therapy at home reduces infusion burden; restaging imaging at an academic center introduces modest travel.

events.label: Abiraterone + prednisone initiated post-chemo
events.description: Second-line ARPI initiated following mixed docetaxel response. Patient transitions back to oral therapy at home.

events.label: Second opinion at NCI cancer center
events.description: First visit to an NCI-designated cancer center. Academic oncologist reviews case and mentions emerging investigational options.

journey.narrative_description: PSMA-PET imaging is introduced for the first time. New lesions are identified. Terry begins to understand the systemic, multifocal nature of their disease more viscerally. The imaging itself — its specificity and the clarity of what it shows — is both clarifying and frightening.
journey.quote: Seeing it on the scan like that — all those lit-up spots — made it real in a way the numbers never did.
sentiment_reasons: Visual confirmation of multifocal disease is more emotionally impactful than PSA numbers alone
PSMA-PET availability only at specialized centers requires meaningful travel and overnight arrangements
PSMA positivity is a prerequisite for future Lu-177 therapy — terry begins to understand its role in the treatment pathway

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The visual reality of metastatic burden on PSMA-PET is viscerally confronting even for patients who intellectually understood their diagnosis.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Nuclear medicine and academic oncology teams are seen as precise and competent; the technology itself inspires a degree of confidence.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry becomes more informed about PSMA biology and its relevance to future therapeutic options.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: PSMA-PET is only available at select centers, requiring significant travel; coverage is inconsistent and some patients pay out-of-pocket.

events.label: ⁶⁸Ga-PSMA-11 PET-CT performed
events.description: PSMA-PET imaging reveals multifocal PSMA-positive bone and lymph node disease. Nuclear medicine physician joins care team.

events.label: Insurance denial for PSMA-PET — appeal required
events.description: Initial insurance claim for PSMA-PET denied. Academic center submits appeal; patient pays out-of-pocket pending resolution.

journey.stage: Treatment Exhaustion & Declining Quality of Life
journey.narrative_description: Second ARPI progresses within 10–14 months. Bone pain becomes a persistent daily reality requiring opioid management. Cabazitaxel chemotherapy is initiated. The caregiver role intensifies significantly. Palliative care is formally introduced — a transition terry experiences as both practical relief and an existential signal.
journey.quote: The pain is always there now. I hate the opioids but I hate the pain more.
sentiment_reasons: Persistent bone pain is a constant physical reminder of disease progression and reduces quality of life acutely
Opioid initiation represents a new, stigmatized treatment category that many patients resist
Introduction of palliative care is correctly interpreted as a shift toward symptom management over curative intent

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Chronic pain, disease progression, and caregiver burden converge to produce the most negative emotional state to date.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The palliative care team is a positive addition, but the overall care team is growing large and harder to navigate.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Pain management complexity and opioid side effect management leave terry feeling less in control of their body.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Cabazitaxel infusions resume infusion center visits; pain management specialist may require travel; prior auths for controlled substances add friction.

events.label: abiraterone progression confirmed — new bone lesions
events.description: PSA rises above 200 ng/mL and new bone lesions appear on imaging. Treatment line exhausted; transition to cabazitaxel planned.

events.label: Palliative care team formally introduced
events.description: Palliative care team joins the care team for symptom management, pain control, and advance care planning support.

events.label: Prior authorization delays for opioid prescriptions
events.description: Controlled substance prior authorizations add administrative friction at each refill cycle. Patient experiences gaps in pain coverage.

journey.stage: Treatment Exhaustion & Declining Quality of Life
journey.narrative_description: Palliative radiation is administered to painful spinal and pelvic lesions. Daily fractions require reliable transportation — an increasing challenge as physical function declines. Home health aide is considered. Social worker takes on a more central coordination role.
journey.quote: Getting to radiation every day takes everything I have. I need help now.
sentiment_reasons: Daily radiation fractions are exhausting for a patient with declining ECOG performance status
Transportation dependence is a major dignity and autonomy loss for independent patients
Palliative rather than curative intent of radiation is emotionally significant even when left unstated

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Physical decline combined with transportation dependence and palliative framing sustain a deeply negative emotional state.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Radiation oncologist's palliative expertise and social worker support are valued; trust is maintained through visible practical help.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry has little agency over treatment decisions at this stage; care is increasingly directed by symptom management needs.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Daily radiation requires transportation infrastructure terry increasingly cannot self-provide; social work transport assistance is essential.

events.label: Hospital transport assistance coordinated by social worker
events.description: Social worker arranges non-emergency medical transport for daily palliative radiation fractions as patient's driving capacity declines.

events.label: Palliative radiation to spine and pelvis completed
events.description: Focused palliative radiation to symptomatic spinal and pelvic lesions provides temporary pain relief.

journey.stage: Treatment Exhaustion & Declining Quality of Life
journey.narrative_description: Lu-177 PSMA (Pluvicto) is initiated as a fourth-line option. It offers 6–9 months of PSA stabilization before resistance emerges. Terry experiences this treatment as both hopeful and logistically demanding — authorized treatment centers are distant, requiring extended travel every 6 weeks.
journey.quote: This one feels different — more targeted, more hopeful. But getting there and back each time is hard.
sentiment_reasons: Lu-177 carries genuine hope as a PSMA-targeted approach validated by clinical trial data
The logistical burden of authorized treatment center travel every 6 weeks strains caregiver and household resources
Brief PSA response followed by re-escalation produces a classic hope-disappointment cycle

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Temporary optimism from treatment response is balanced against the physical and logistical demands of the regimen.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The nuclear medicine team at the authorized center is experienced and communicative; trial coordinator introduces future options.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry has developed a more sophisticated understanding of their disease biology and actively engages with treatment rationale.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Round-trip travel to an authorized Pluvicto center every 6 weeks, combined with radiation safety protocols, is a major logistical undertaking.

events.label: Pluvicto cycle 1 administered at authorized center
events.description: First Pluvicto infusion administered. PSA begins to decline. Novartis patient assistance program enrollment initiated for cost coverage.

events.label: PSA re-escalation after initial Pluvicto response
events.description: After 4–6 cycles, PSA begins rising again. The trial coordinator formally introduces investigational options for the first time.

events.label: Trial coordinator introduces investigational options
events.description: Authorized center trial coordinator mentions available investigational studies for PSMA-positive mARMR-PC following Lu-177 progression.

journey.stage: Treatment Exhaustion & Declining Quality of Life
journey.narrative_description: Caregiver burden intensifies significantly. Terry reduces work or stops entirely. Social withdrawal, depression, and financial toxicity compound physical decline. An antidepressant is initiated. Telemedicine begins supplementing in-person visits to reduce transportation burden.
journey.quote: I've stopped going to things. It's too hard, and I don't want anyone to see me like this.
sentiment_reasons: Social withdrawal and depression are now clinically significant and directly impair quality of life
Financial toxicity — lifetime out-of-pocket costs exceeding $150,000 — creates existential household stress
Caregiver burden has reached a level that strains family relationships and terry's sense of being a burden

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Depression, social withdrawal, and physical decline converge into the most emotionally depleted state of the journey.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Psychiatry/psychology addition to the care team is positive; the social worker remains a trusted anchor.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry feels increasingly passive in treatment decisions as options narrow and energy for self-advocacy diminishes.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Telemedicine supplements in-person visits; disability filing and estate planning add bureaucratic burden to an already exhausted household.

events.label: Clinical depression diagnosed — SSRI initiated
events.description: Oncology team refers patient to psychiatry. SSRI initiated alongside melatonin for sleep disruption.

events.label: Caregiver support group referral
events.description: Social worker refers primary caregiver to a prostate cancer caregiver support group. Telemedicine visits introduced to reduce transportation burden.

journey.stage: Treatment Exhaustion & Declining Quality of Life
journey.narrative_description: The oncologist conducts a frank goals-of-care conversation. Standard treatment options are characterized as exhausted or near-exhausted. Terry and family begin processing what comes next while simultaneously being introduced to clinical trial eligibility for the first time as a formal next step.
journey.quote: She was honest with me — I appreciated that. Now I have to figure out what matters most to me.
sentiment_reasons: A frank, compassionate goals-of-care discussion is experienced as both frightening and deeply respectful
Advance directive conversations engage existential questions terry has been deferring
Clinical trial introduction offers a concrete forward path that partly offsets the gravity of the conversation

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The goals-of-care conversation is inherently distressing but terry's trust in the oncologist softens its impact.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Honest, compassionate communication from the oncologist significantly increases trust — transparency is valued highly by this patient.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry gains agency through advance directive completion and active participation in defining care goals.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Estate planning and advance directive preparation engage financial and legal systems; trial site logistics planning begins.

events.label: Advance directive and estate planning initiated
events.description: Patient completes advance directive with support from social worker and family. Financial planner consulted regarding medical debt.

events.label: Trial coordinator formally introduced to patient and family
events.description: Clinical trial coordinator presents the ABO-701 study as a strong fit given PSMA-positive status, ECOG 1, and prior therapy history.

journey.narrative_description: The oncologist identifies the ABO-701 trial as a strong clinical fit given PSMA-positive disease, preserved ECOG 1 status, and prior therapy history. The trial coordinator becomes the primary contact. Hope re-enters terry's emotional landscape for the first time in months.
journey.quote: Finally — something that was designed for exactly where I am.
sentiment_reasons: Precision match between trial eligibility criteria and terry's actual disease profile is experienced as validation
The trial coordinator's communication style and knowledge instill confidence in the investigational pathway
Sponsor-covered procedures reduce financial anxiety associated with continuing standard care

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Trial identification reactivates hope — a genuine emotional uplift after months of decline.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust expands to include the trial team; the principal investigator's expertise adds a new high-credibility clinical voice.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Patient begins actively researching the trial, CAR-T mechanisms, and published early-phase data.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Trial site may require extended stays near academic center; family logistics planning adds planning burden.

events.label: ABO-701 trial identified as strong eligibility match
events.description: Medical oncologist and trial coordinator confirm terry meets all ABO-701 inclusion criteria. Formal trial introduction meeting scheduled.

events.label: Nonprofit travel assistance programs identified
events.description: Trial coordinator connects patient with nonprofit travel assistance resources to offset lodging and transportation costs during the trial.
events.platform: ZERO Prostate Cancer / Patient Advocate Foundation

journey.narrative_description: Terry and family independently research ABO-701. They navigate ClinicalTrials.gov, patient advocacy forums, and prostate cancer communities where others have enrolled in similar trials. Hope and skepticism oscillate. Terry emerges with more questions and a sharper sense of the risks they are weighing.
journey.quote: Every forum post I read sends me in a different direction. I need to figure out what I actually believe.
sentiment_reasons: Independent research increases medical self-efficacy but also surfaces risks and uncertainties not emphasized by the clinical team
Patient advocacy communities provide lived experience that is both reassuring and sobering
ClinicalTrials.gov is complex and difficult to interpret without clinical training — a source of frustration

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Oscillation between hope and skepticism produces a near-neutral emotional valence with high internal volatility.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Independent information-gathering temporarily distances terry from pure reliance on clinical team framing.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Actively engaging with primary trial data, forums, and advocacy resources represents the highest point of patient-driven health literacy.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Travel and lodging cost estimation ($5,000–15,000) adds financial planning stress; crowdfunding and foundation grants are explored.

events.label: ClinicalTrials.gov research on ABO-701
events.description: Patient and family review the full ABO-701 protocol on ClinicalTrials.gov. Inclusion/exclusion criteria, endpoint definitions, and primary SAE risks reviewed.

events.label: Online prostate cancer community — trial participant accounts
events.description: Patient connects with others who have enrolled in similar PSMA-targeted CAR-T trials. Firsthand accounts are mixed — some inspiring, some sobering.

journey.narrative_description: Terry is formally referred to the trial site. Screening confirms eligibility — PSMA-PET qualifies, labs are within threshold, and performance status is acceptable. The full trial team is introduced. Terry experiences a procedural momentum that feels purposeful after months of decline.
journey.quote: They said I qualified. After everything, I actually qualified.
sentiment_reasons: Screening confirmation is a meaningful positive outcome after a period of uncertainty
Meeting the full trial team — PI, sub-investigators, nurses, coordinators — builds confidence and reduces the unknown
Sponsor-covered screening procedures remove a financial barrier and demonstrate sponsor commitment

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Eligibility confirmation restores a sense of forward momentum and purpose.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The principal investigator and trial team inspire high confidence through their depth of protocol knowledge.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Patient actively reviews screening results and asks informed questions about the protocol.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: First visit to trial site allows caregiver and patient to assess practical logistics of repeated visits and lodging needs.

events.label: PSMA-PET qualifying scan performed at trial site
events.description: Baseline PSMA-PET CT confirms qualifying PSMA expression above liver parenchyma threshold in multiple lesions. Sponsor-covered.

events.label: Full trial team introduced to patient and caregiver
events.description: PI, sub-investigators, trial nurses, research coordinators, and pharmacist are formally introduced. Expectations and protocol timeline reviewed.

journey.narrative_description: Informed consent is completed. Terry weighs manufacturing failure risk, CRS toxicity probability, and non-response likelihood before signing. Leukapheresis is performed and ABO-701 manufacturing is initiated. The 7–10 day manufacturing window requires terry and caregiver to remain near the trial site.
journey.quote: Signing that form was the hardest thing I've done. But I've run out of other options — and this one actually makes sense.

inflection_detail.label: Manufacturing & Consent Inflection
inflection_detail.summary: The consent-to-leukapheresis window is the highest-stakes decision point in the trial journey. Two paths diverge based on manufacturing success and patient readiness.

paths.label: Successful Manufacturing & Enrollment
paths.outcome: ABO-701 product manufactured within the 7–10 day window. Patient remains clinically stable during the wait. Day 1 dosing proceeds on schedule. Terry and caregiver experience the manufacturing wait as tense but manageable, supported by trial site staff and coordinated temporary housing.

paths.label: Manufacturing Failure or Clinical Deterioration
paths.outcome: ABO-701 manufacturing fails or product does not meet release specifications, or terry's performance status declines during the manufacturing window, making Day 1 dosing unsafe. Terry and caregiver face a devastating setback after having committed fully to the trial pathway. Re-manufacturing may be attempted if terry remains eligible.
paths.next_step: Manufacturing restart or compassionate care transition

key_risk_factors: Manufacturing failure rate for PSMA-targeted CAR-T products
Patient ECOG performance status deterioration during washout and manufacturing window
Caregiver exhaustion and logistical breakdown during extended site stay
inflection_analysis.sponsor_opportunity: Proactive caregiver coordination, manufacturing status transparency, and on-site patient support services can meaningfully reduce dropout and anxiety at this inflection.

sponsor_actions.label: Real-time manufacturing status updates to patient and caregiver
sponsor_actions.rationale: Transparency about the manufacturing process reduces uncertainty-driven anxiety during the 7–10 day wait.

sponsor_actions.label: Coordinated temporary housing and caregiver support during manufacturing window
sponsor_actions.rationale: Logistical support prevents caregiver breakdown and patient dropout during the highest-stakes window of the trial.

sentiment_reasons: Informed consent forces explicit confrontation with manufacturing failure risk, CRS toxicity, and non-response probability
The manufacturing wait period — outside terry's control — generates significant anticipatory anxiety
Successful leukapheresis completion is experienced as a milestone and partial commitment to the trial pathway

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The consent and leukapheresis experience is emotionally complex — hope and fear coexist in near-equal measure.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The trial team's preparation, transparency, and responsiveness during this period are critical to sustaining trust.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terry has done extensive preparation and engages with the consent process as an informed participant.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Extended stay near the trial site during manufacturing requires temporary housing, caregiver coordination, and financial resources.

events.label: Informed consent signed — all prior systemic therapies washed out
events.description: Patient signs informed consent after extended discussion of manufacturing risks, CRS probability, and non-response likelihood. Washout of all prior systemic therapies initiated.

events.label: Leukapheresis performed — ABO-701 manufacturing initiated
events.description: Apheresis performed at trial site. T-cell collection successful. ABO-701 manufacturing initiated with 7–10 day expected production window.

events.label: Manufacturing window — patient and caregiver remain at site
events.description: Patient and caregiver must remain near the trial site during the manufacturing window. Temporary housing coordinated. Caregiver anxiety peaks.

profile.name: Rachel M.
profile.occupation: High school English teacher (currently working with reduced schedule)
profile.preference: Seeks disease-modifying gene therapy; prefers academic center care with cardiogenetics expertise
profile.diagnosed: DCM diagnosed 2 years ago; BAG3 pathogenic variant confirmed 8 months ago via cascade testing
profile.distance to care: 4.5 hours to nearest BAG3 gene therapy trial site (UCSD or MUSC)
profile.quote: They told me my heart was failing but couldn't tell me why. The genetic test changed everything — now I finally know what I'm fighting, but I also know my kids might be fighting it too.
profile.bio_1: Rachel was preparing for the spring semester when she began experiencing shortness of breath walking between classrooms and unusual fatigue by early afternoon. Her PCP attributed the symptoms to stress and deconditioning. Six months later, after progressive worsening and two near-syncope episodes, an echocardiogram revealed an LVEF of 32% with left ventricular dilation. She was diagnosed with idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy at a community hospital. No genetic testing was offered or discussed.
profile.bio_2: Rachel is analytically minded — her career teaching close reading has trained her to scrutinize evidence carefully. Since receiving her BAG3 diagnosis through cascade testing triggered by her brother's hospitalization, she has become deeply engaged with the DCM Foundation and online BAG3 communities. She often knows about emerging gene therapy trials before her local cardiologist. She is meticulous in evaluating clinical evidence, but that same precision amplifies her anxiety about what the data means for her prognosis and her children's futures. She and her husband have begun the difficult process of deciding whether and when to test their two children (ages 8 and 5) for the family variant.
profile.goal1: Access a disease-modifying gene therapy that addresses the genetic root cause of her DCM, not just symptom management with GDMT.
profile.goal2: Ensure her two children receive appropriate genetic counseling and cardiac screening — on a timeline and in a manner that protects their emotional wellbeing.
profile.goal3: Maintain her teaching career and physical independence as long as possible, preserving normalcy for her family.
profile.barrier1: Geographic distance from academic trial sites — the nearest RP-A701 or ALXN2350 site is 4.5+ hours away, requiring her husband to take time off work and her mother to manage childcare for multi-day visits.
profile.barrier2: Uncertainty about whether her current clinical profile (LVEF 34%, NYHA Class II, ICD in place) makes her 'sick enough' to justify the risks of a first-in-human Phase 1 gene therapy or 'too well' to prioritize over sicker patients.
profile.barrier3: Fear that enrolling in one gene therapy trial permanently excludes her from future, potentially superior AAV-based therapies — given that all three competing trials (Rocket, Alexion, Affinia) exclude patients with prior gene therapy exposure.

journey.narrative_description: Rachel begins experiencing shortness of breath climbing stairs at school and unusual fatigue by mid-afternoon. She attributes it to the demands of the school year, poor sleep, and the logistics of parenting two young children. A visit to her PCP results in basic labs, a chest X-ray, and a TSH — all unremarkable. She is told to reduce stress, exercise more, and return in three months if symptoms persist. No cardiac workup is initiated.
journey.quote: I thought I was just out of shape. Everyone kept telling me it was stress. My husband said I needed to sleep more. My doctor said I needed to exercise. Nobody said 'let's look at your heart.'

events.label: PCP Attributes Symptoms to Stress and Deconditioning
events.description: Rachel's PCP performs basic labs and a chest X-ray but does not order an echocardiogram or refer to cardiology. Exertional dyspnea in a 36-year-old woman without traditional cardiovascular risk factors does not trigger a cardiac workup in the community primary care setting. Rachel leaves the visit reassured but undiagnosed.
events.tooltip: PCP does not pursue cardiac workup for exertional dyspnea in a 36-year-old woman — standard labs and chest X-ray are normal. Diagnosis delayed.

events.label: Non-Ischemic DCM Screening Prompt for Young Adults with Unexplained Dyspnea
events.description: An EHR-integrated clinical decision support tool that prompts PCPs to consider echocardiography when patients under 45 present with unexplained exertional dyspnea — particularly women, who are systematically under-referred for cardiac workup — would intercept Rachel at her first visit. The tool would flag that non-ischemic DCM is the most common cause of heart failure in young adults and that standard labs and chest X-ray are insufficient to exclude it.
events.tooltip: EHR-integrated prompt for echocardiography in young adults with unexplained dyspnea — intercepts the systematic under-investigation of cardiac causes in women under 45.

sentiment_reasons: Symptoms are subtle enough to rationalize as lifestyle-related — Rachel herself participates in the minimization
PCP visit provides reassurance but no actionable diagnostic pathway
The gender-specific pattern of cardiac symptom dismissal in young women is invisible to Rachel at this stage

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Mild concern buffered by the normalizing reassurance from her PCP and husband.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust is moderate — PCP is responsive and thorough within her framework, but the framework excludes cardiac evaluation.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel has no vocabulary for interpreting cardiac symptoms in herself; she defaults to the stress narrative.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Life is manageable; symptoms are annoying but not yet disruptive to teaching or parenting.

treatment_changes.note: PCP prescribes for edema pending cardiology referral

journey.narrative_description: Over the next six months, Rachel's symptoms worsen materially. She develops lower extremity edema, can no longer walk the hallways between classes without stopping to rest, and has two episodes of near-syncope — one while supervising students in the cafeteria. She returns to her PCP, who this time orders an echocardiogram. The results show an LVEF of 32% with left ventricular dilation. Rachel is urgently referred to cardiology. The referring nurse's facial expression when reading the echo report is the first signal that something is seriously wrong.
journey.quote: When they said my heart was only pumping at 32 percent, I didn't even know what that meant. I just saw the nurse's face change. That's when I understood.

events.label: Six-Month Diagnostic Delay — Echo Not Ordered Until Return Visit
events.description: Rachel presented with exertional dyspnea six months ago. An echocardiogram was not ordered until she returned with worsening symptoms including edema and pre-syncope. The delay is consistent with documented patterns of cardiac under-investigation in young women presenting to primary care.
events.tooltip: 6-month gap between first presentation with dyspnea and first echocardiogram — consistent with systematic under-referral for cardiac workup in women under 45.

events.label: Echo Reveals LVEF 32% — Urgent Cardiology Referral
events.description: Echocardiogram shows LVEF 32% with LV dilation. Rachel is urgently referred to cardiology the same day. The finding reframes six months of dismissed symptoms as undiagnosed heart failure.
events.tooltip: LVEF 32% with LV dilation on first echo — urgent cardiology referral initiated. Six months of symptoms now recontextualized as undiagnosed heart failure.

sentiment_reasons: The echo result is a shock — Rachel had no cognitive preparation for heart failure as a diagnosis
Retrospective recognition that her symptoms were cardiac, not stress-related, generates anger at the six-month delay
The urgency of the referral signals medical seriousness she was not prepared for

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Fear replaces reassurance; Rachel is processing the gap between what she was told and what is actually happening.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in the PCP erodes — if this was visible on an echo, why wasn't one ordered six months ago?
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel feels blindsided; she has no vocabulary for what is happening to her heart.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Urgent cardiology referral, new appointment cascade, and near-syncope episodes at work begin disrupting her professional and family routines.

treatment_changes.note: First formal HF regimen initiated during 3-day admission
First formal HF regimen initiated during 3-day admission
Increased from 20 mg during admission for acute decompensation

journey.narrative_description: Before her scheduled cardiology appointment, Rachel's dyspnea worsens acutely. Her husband brings her to the ED after she cannot complete a sentence without stopping to breathe. She is admitted for three days with acute decompensated heart failure. IV diuresis reduces her fluid overload and stabilizes her symptoms. She is discharged on her first formal HF medication regimen: lisinopril, metoprolol succinate, and oral furosemide. The admission is her first direct encounter with the hospital system as a cardiac patient. She is 36 years old. She cries twice — once when she sees her children in the hospital waiting room, and once alone in her room at 2 AM when she finally processes what is happening to her.
journey.quote: They put me in a hospital gown and started an IV and suddenly it was real. Not 'your echo looks concerning' real. ICU-down-the-hall real.

events.label: Acute Decompensation Before Scheduled Cardiology Visit
events.description: Rachel decompensates before her outpatient cardiology appointment can occur. The acute presentation — requiring inpatient admission, IV diuresis, and emergent workup — represents a failure of the care transition from PCP referral to outpatient cardiology. A more compressed referral timeline or interim phone-based triage might have intercepted her before decompensation.
events.tooltip: Decompensation occurs in the gap between PCP referral and first cardiology appointment — acute HF admission precedes outpatient evaluation.

events.label: IV Diuresis and Stabilization — HF Regimen Initiated
events.description: Three-day admission achieves clinical stabilization. Rachel is discharged on lisinopril, metoprolol succinate, and oral furosemide — her first formal HF regimen. The inpatient cardiology team is attentive and communicates the diagnosis clearly for the first time. Rachel leaves with a confirmed diagnosis, a medication regimen, and a follow-up appointment in two weeks.
events.tooltip: Inpatient stabilization with IV diuresis. Discharged on first formal HF regimen — lisinopril, metoprolol, furosemide. First honest diagnostic conversation.

sentiment_reasons: Hospital admission makes the severity of her illness undeniable — the abstraction of an abnormal echo becomes a concrete medical crisis
Seeing her children in the waiting room forces Rachel to confront the stakes in human, not clinical, terms
The inpatient team's clarity about her diagnosis is a relief even as the diagnosis itself is devastating

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Terror and grief — the admission removes all remaining ambiguity about the seriousness of her condition.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The inpatient cardiology team earns trust through clarity and competence; the PCP's six-month delay is now starkly visible.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel is completely dependent on the clinical team during the acute phase; her sense of control is at its lowest point.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Three-day hospitalization, husband taking emergency leave, mother managing childcare — the family logistics system is under acute stress.

journey.step: Idiopathic DCM Diagnosis — No Genetic Testing

treatment_changes.note: Outpatient uptitration
Outpatient uptitration
MRA added; genetic testing not discussed

journey.narrative_description: Rachel undergoes a comprehensive cardiac workup at her community hospital: repeat echocardiography, cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement, coronary angiography (ruling out ischemic disease), and standard labs including BNP and thyroid function. The findings confirm non-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy. No specific etiology is identified — it is classified as 'idiopathic.' She is started on sacubitril/valsartan, carvedilol, and spironolactone. The cardiologist discusses ICD implantation for primary prevention. Genetic testing is not mentioned, discussed, or offered at any point during the diagnostic workup — consistent with the <1% real-world genetic testing rate for DCM in routine US clinical practice.
journey.quote: They said my heart is enlarged and weak, but they can't tell me why. I asked if it was genetic. He said 'probably not — you're young and otherwise healthy.' That was the end of the conversation.

events.label: Genetic Testing Not Offered or Discussed During DCM Workup
events.description: Despite AHA/ACC/HFSA guidelines recommending genetic evaluation for patients with non-ischemic DCM, Rachel's community cardiologist does not order, discuss, or offer genetic testing. When Rachel asks whether her condition could be genetic, the cardiologist dismisses the possibility based on her age and absence of obvious family history — without taking a structured three-generation pedigree. This omission is the single most consequential missed opportunity in her entire journey.
events.tooltip: Genetic testing not offered despite guideline recommendations. Cardiologist dismisses genetic etiology without structured family history. <1% of DCM patients receive genetic testing in routine US practice.

events.label: GDMT Initiated — Entresto, Carvedilol, Spironolactone
events.description: Rachel is started on guideline-directed medical therapy: sacubitril/valsartan (Entresto), carvedilol, and spironolactone. The cardiology team demonstrates competence in HF management and explains each medication clearly.
events.tooltip: GDMT initiated competently — Entresto, carvedilol, spironolactone. Cardiology team is skilled at HF management; the gap is upstream in etiologic evaluation.

events.label: Genetic Testing Prompt Embedded in DCM Diagnostic Pathway
events.description: A clinical decision support alert within the EHR — triggered when a non-ischemic DCM diagnosis is documented in a patient under 60 — would prompt the cardiologist to consider genetic evaluation and offer a structured three-generation family history assessment. The alert would include a direct link to order a cardiomyopathy gene panel and a referral pathway to genetic counseling. At the estimated <1% real-world genetic testing rate for DCM, even a modest improvement in testing uptake would dramatically expand the identifiable BAG3 population — the rate-limiting step for all three competing gene therapy trials.
events.tooltip: EHR-triggered genetic testing prompt for non-ischemic DCM diagnoses under 60 — addresses the <1% testing rate that constrains the entire BAG3 gene therapy recruitment pipeline.
events.partner: DCM Foundation / Genetic Cardiomyopathy Awareness Consortium

events.label: No Patient-Facing Resource Explaining Genetic Testing for DCM at Diagnosis
events.description: At the point of DCM diagnosis — when Rachel is actively asking 'why?' — there is no patient-facing resource in her hospital, cardiology office, or discharge materials that explains the role of genetic testing in DCM, the guideline recommendations for it, or how to request it. The DCM Foundation and GCAC resources exist but are not surfaced in clinical settings. Rachel's question about genetics is answered by a single dismissive sentence from her cardiologist rather than an informed discussion.
events.tooltip: Missing resource: no patient-facing genetic testing education in clinical DCM workflow — terry asks the right question but receives a dismissive answer with no supporting materials.

sentiment_reasons: Receiving a life-altering diagnosis at 36 with no identifiable cause triggers grief and disorientation — 'idiopathic' feels like a non-answer
Rachel's instinct to ask about genetics is correct but is dismissed without investigation — she won't know this was a missed opportunity for another 14 months
The absence of genetic testing is invisible to Rachel at this stage; she trusts the system to have asked the right questions

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Grief dominates — Rachel is processing a new identity as a heart failure patient at 36. The lack of etiologic clarity amplifies the sense of randomness and injustice.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The cardiology team is competent at HF management, which sustains trust; the genetic testing omission will only erode trust retroactively.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel begins learning HF terminology and medication management, building functional health literacy within the GDMT framework.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Multiple new medications, follow-up appointments, activity restrictions, and schedule accommodations at work disrupt daily routines.

treatment_changes.note: ARNI switch replaces ACE inhibitor
Beta-blocker class switch
Reduced as fluid status improves with ARNI
Single-chamber ICD implanted for primary prevention

journey.narrative_description: Over the next eight months, Rachel's medications are uptitrated to target doses. Dapagliflozin is added. Her symptoms stabilize and her LVEF improves modestly to 34%. She receives a single-chamber ICD for primary prevention given persistently reduced EF. She adjusts to life with the device — learning which movements trigger false motion alerts, negotiating airport security, and explaining the visible lump under her clavicle to colleagues. Her cardiologist tells her she is 'doing well on medical therapy.' Rachel knows this means her heart is still fundamentally impaired — GDMT is managing her symptoms, not reversing her disease.
journey.quote: The ICD is supposed to save my life. But wearing it every day reminds me that my heart could stop without warning. I touch it through my shirt during faculty meetings. Nobody notices. I notice every time.

events.label: LVEF Improves to 34% — Stable on Optimized GDMT
events.description: After 8 months of GDMT optimization including the addition of dapagliflozin, Rachel's LVEF improves from 32% to 34%. Symptoms are manageable. She returns to teaching on a slightly reduced schedule. The improvement is real but modest — she remains firmly in the reduced-EF category.
events.tooltip: LVEF improves 32% → 34% on optimized GDMT. Functional improvement allows return to teaching. Still reduced-EF with ICD in place.

events.label: ICD Implantation — Primary Prevention
events.description: Single-chamber ICD implanted for primary prevention. Procedure is uncomplicated. Rachel adjusts to the device physically but experiences ongoing psychological burden — hyperawareness of the device, anxiety about appropriate shocks, and the constant reminder of cardiac vulnerability.
events.tooltip: ICD implanted for primary prevention. Physically uncomplicated; psychologically complex. The device is simultaneously protective and a persistent source of illness identity.

sentiment_reasons: Physical stabilization provides genuine relief — Rachel can teach and parent with manageable symptoms
ICD implantation is simultaneously protective and psychologically burdensome — a device that saves her life by reminding her she might die
The 'doing well on medical therapy' framing from her cardiologist is accurate but insufficient — Rachel knows GDMT treats symptoms, not the unknown cause

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Neutral — the genuine improvement in physical function balances the emotional weight of device dependency and diagnostic uncertainty.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in the cardiology team improves as they demonstrate sustained competence in HF management and medication optimization.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel develops real expertise in reading her symptoms, managing medications, tracking vitals, and adapting her daily routines.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Routine stabilizes — ICD follow-ups and cardiology visits are manageable. Work accommodations are in place.

treatment_changes.note: Uptitrated to target dose
SGLT2i added to complete quad therapy
Diuretic discontinued — fluid status stable on quad therapy

journey.narrative_description: With quad therapy now complete and her LVEF stable at 34%, Rachel enters a sustained plateau. She has adapted her teaching schedule, learned to read her body's signals, and built a functional rhythm around cardiology follow-ups, ICD checks, and medication management. The diagnosis is no longer a crisis — it is a chronic condition she manages with discipline and resignation. She does not get worse. She does not get better. She knows 'stable' is the clinical ceiling of what her current treatment can achieve. The question she cannot answer is whether this plateau will hold, or whether the BAG3 natural history she does not yet know about is already accumulating beneath the surface.
journey.quote: Stable sounds like good news. And I know it is. But stable also means this is it — this is the best it gets without something changing. I'm a 37-year-old woman whose heart is as good as it's going to get on these pills.

events.label: Therapeutic Ceiling Reached — GDMT Cannot Reverse Underlying Disease
events.description: Rachel's LVEF has plateaued at 34% despite fully optimized quad therapy. Her cardiologist confirms there are no remaining medication adjustments to make. The plateau is genuinely good news — she is not deteriorating — but it is also the system signaling that it has nothing more to offer her within its current framework. She is 37 years old at her treatment ceiling.
events.tooltip: Quad therapy complete; LVEF stable at 34%. No remaining GDMT adjustments — therapeutic ceiling reached. Disease is managed, not treated.

events.label: Rachel Adapts to Chronic Illness Identity — Functional Stability
events.description: Rachel develops the practical and psychological competencies of an experienced chronic illness patient: she tracks her fluid weight daily, adjusts her activity level proactively, manages her medications without reminders, and has negotiated sustainable accommodations at work. The stability is earned through discipline, not luck.
events.tooltip: Rachel achieves functional stability through disciplined self-management — daily weight monitoring, proactive activity adjustment, sustainable work accommodations.

sentiment_reasons: The plateau is functionally comfortable but emotionally ambiguous — 'stable' is not 'better'
Rachel's exceptional health literacy means she understands that GDMT manages symptoms without addressing the unknown underlying cause
Without a genetic diagnosis at this stage, there is no horizon beyond the plateau — no next intervention, no disease-modifying pathway

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Cautious, resigned positivity — Rachel has found a workable equilibrium that she knows is fragile and finite.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in the cardiology team is high within the GDMT framework; the team is doing everything the standard of care offers.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel is at her highest self-efficacy yet — she manages her disease competently within the framework she has been given.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Routines are stable; the administrative and logistical burden of chronic disease management has become normalized.

journey.narrative_description: Rachel's younger brother Marcus, age 34, is admitted to an academic medical center with acute decompensated heart failure. He has no prior cardiac history. His echocardiogram shows an LVEF of 22% with severe biventricular dilation. He requires inotropic support for 72 hours. The coincidence of two siblings under 40 with DCM immediately triggers genetic suspicion in the treating HF cardiologist — a physician with cardiogenetics training. A comprehensive cardiomyopathy gene panel is ordered for Marcus before he leaves the ICU. Rachel receives the call from her mother at 6:15 AM on a Tuesday. She teaches four classes that day without telling anyone.
journey.quote: When Marcus collapsed, my first thought was 'it's in our blood.' My second thought was about my kids. My third thought — and this is the one I'm ashamed of — was: why didn't anyone test me?

events.label: Second Sibling with DCM Under 40 — Familial Pattern Unmistakable
events.description: Marcus, 34, presents with acute decompensated HF and LVEF 22%. No prior cardiac history. Two siblings under 40 with DCM constitutes a clear familial pattern. The academic center's HF cardiologist, who has cardiogenetics training, orders genetic testing immediately. Rachel's community cardiologist did not flag this possibility 14 months earlier.
events.tooltip: Brother hospitalized with acute HF and LVEF 22% at age 34. Academic center orders genetic panel immediately — Rachel's community team never considered genetics 14 months prior.

events.label: Retroactive Trust Rupture — Why Wasn't I Tested?
events.description: The academic center's immediate genetic testing of Marcus — contrasted with Rachel's 14-month-old ungenotyped 'idiopathic' diagnosis — creates a retroactive trust rupture with her community cardiology team. She asked about genetics at diagnosis. She was told it was unlikely. The academic system recognized the need instantly.
events.tooltip: Academic center orders genetic panel within hours of Marcus's admission. Rachel's community team dismissed her genetics question 14 months ago. The gap in standard of care is now visible.

sentiment_reasons: Sibling's acute presentation is terrifying — it feels like a preview of Rachel's own possible trajectory
The familial pattern makes the genetic hypothesis undeniable in a way it wasn't when Rachel was the only case
Fear immediately shifts to her children — the implications multiply exponentially from personal to generational

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Terror and anticipatory grief dominate — this is no longer abstract. The threat is visible in her brother's ICU bed.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in Rachel's own cardiology team drops to its lowest point — the academic center's immediate genetic testing exposes the gap.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel feels powerless despite having managed her own disease competently — she couldn't foresee or prevent this.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Emotional crisis compounds with travel to her brother's hospital, childcare coordination, and the pretense of normalcy at work.

journey.narrative_description: Marcus's genetic panel returns positive for a pathogenic variant in BAG3 — a loss-of-function mutation in a gene critical for cardiac protein quality control. The academic center's genetic counselor contacts Rachel directly and offers targeted cascade testing for the family variant. Rachel's result comes back positive within two weeks. For the first time in 14 months, her DCM has a molecular name: BAG3-associated dilated cardiomyopathy. The genetic counselor explains autosomal dominant inheritance — each of Rachel's two children has a 50% chance of carrying the variant. Rachel experiences a complex, simultaneous mixture of validation (finally, a cause), grief (it's genetic, it's permanent), and dread (her children may carry it). She begins researching BAG3 specifically that evening and dicovers the DCM Foundation, the GCAC, and — within 48 hsours — the existence of three AAV gene therapy programs targeting BAG3.
journey.quote: Knowing the 'why' was a relief and a devastation at the same time. I finally had an answer, but the answer was that it's written into my DNA — and maybe into my children's. I cried in the parking lot of the genetics office. Then I went home and started Googling BAG3 gene therapy.

events.label: BAG3 Pathogenic Variant Confirmed in Both Siblings
events.description: Marcus's gene panel identifies a pathogenic BAG3 loss-of-function variant. Rachel's targeted cascade test confirms she carries the same variant. Her DCM is reclassified from 'idiopathic' to BAG3-associated. The diagnosis provides molecular clarity but introduces heritability — each child has a 50% probability of carrying the variant.
events.tooltip: BAG3 pathogenic variant confirmed in both siblings. DCM reclassified from 'idiopathic' to BAG3-associated. Autosomal dominant — 50% transmission risk per child.

events.label: Rachel Discovers DCM Foundation, GCAC, and BAG3 Gene Therapy Landscape
events.description: Within 48 hours of her BAG3 confirmation, Rachel finds the DCM Foundation website, the Genetic Cardiomyopathy Awareness Consortium, a BAG3-specific Facebook group, and ClinicalTrials.gov listings for three AAV gene therapy programs: Rocket's RP-A701, Alexion's ALXN2350 (DCMRestore), and Affinia's AFTX-201 (UPBEAT). She creates a comparison spreadsheet of eligibility criteria, trial sites, capsid types, and enrollment timelines.
events.tooltip: Within 48 hours of BAG3 confirmation: finds DCM Foundation, GCAC, BAG3 Facebook group, and three competing gene therapy trial listings. Creates comparison spreadsheet.

events.label: Genetic Diagnosis-to-Trial Navigation Pathway — Automated Matching at Confirmation
events.description: A sponsor-supported digital pathway — triggered when a BAG3 pathogenic variant is confirmed by any clinical lab — that automatically surfaces relevant clinical trial listings, registry enrollment options, and patient community resources would intercept Rachel at her highest moment of motivated information-seeking. Rather than relying on her to independently discover ClinicalTrials.gov, the DCM Foundation, and individual trial listings, the pathway would present a curated, BAG3-specific next-steps package within the genetic test result delivery workflow.
events.tooltip: Automated BAG3-specific trial and community matching triggered at genetic diagnosis confirmation — intercepts terry at peak information-seeking motivation.
events.partner: DCM Foundation / Clinical genetic testing labs (Invitae, GeneDx, Ambry)

events.label: Community Cardiologist Cannot Contextualize BAG3 Result
events.description: When Rachel brings her BAG3 result to her community cardiologist, he acknowledges the finding but cannot provide BAG3-specific prognostic information, management changes, or trial awareness. He says 'we'll keep doing what we're doing — the medications are working.' For Rachel, this confirms that her local team is managing symptoms competently but cannot engage with the genotype-specific dimension of her disease. She begins considering transferring her primary cardiac care.
events.tooltip: Community cardiologist acknowledges BAG3 result but cannot contextualize it — no management changes, no trial awareness, no BAG3-specific prognostic discussion.

sentiment_reasons: Diagnostic clarity resolves 14 months of 'idiopathic' uncertainty — the relief is genuine but the answer is heavy
BAG3 is rare enough (~2-4% of DCM) that her local cardiologist has no specific expertise — she is now more informed than her treating physician
Autosomal dominant inheritance transforms a personal cardiac diagnosis into a generational family crisis — the stakes multiply

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Ambivalent — the profound relief of finally having a molecular explanation collides with grief about permanence and terror about heritability.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust bifurcates sharply: high trust in the academic genetics team; declining trust in her community cardiologist who cannot engage with the genotype.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel's research capability peaks — she now has a specific target (BAG3) rather than the undifferentiated ocean of 'idiopathic DCM.' Her comparison spreadsheet is the artifact of this shift.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Cascade testing logistics for children, genetic counseling appointments, second-opinion visits, and the emotional labor of the inheritance conversation add substantial burden.

inflection_detail.label: BAG3 Genetic Confirmation — Genotype-Specific Path Fork
inflection_detail.description: The genetic diagnosis transforms Rachel's journey from undifferentiated HF management to a genotype-specific trajectory. This is the critical fork: she can either remain in standard cardiology care with no genotype-informed interventions — where BAG3 is a label in her chart but not an actionable driver of treatment decisions — or enter the emerging BAG3-specific ecosystem of registries, research communities, and gene therapy trials. The fork is driven not by Rachel's motivation (which is uniformly high) but by whether the system surfaces the genotype-specific pathway at the moment of diagnosis. The <1% genetic testing rate means most BAG3 patients never reach this fork at all. Rachel reached it only because her brother was hospitalized at an academic center with cardiogenetics expertise.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Establish a BAG3-specific patient identification and navigation program — partnering with clinical genetic testing laboratories to embed trial awareness and community connection into the genetic result delivery workflow. Every confirmed BAG3 pathogenic variant is a potential trial candidate. The bottleneck is not patient motivation but system-level surfacing of the genotype-to-trial pathway.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Number of BAG3 pathogenic/likely pathogenic variants confirmed annually by major US clinical labs; proportion that result in trial referral or registry enrollment within 6 months; time from BAG3 confirmation to first gene therapy trial contact.

paths.label: Genotype-Informed Engagement — Trial Pipeline Entry
paths.outcome: Rachel connects with the DCM Foundation, enrolls in Alexion's BAG3 natural history study (NCT05981092), transfers her primary cardiac care to an academic center with cardiogenetics expertise, and begins active screening conversations with trial sites for RP-A701 and ALXN2350. Her children undergo predictive testing with age-appropriate genetic counseling. The BAG3 diagnosis becomes the organizing framework for all clinical decisions.
paths.key_driver: Immediate access to genetic counselor with cardiac expertise; awareness of BAG3-specific patient communities and trial landscape; academic cardiologist who integrates genotype into management.

paths.label: Genotype-Blind Continuation — Diagnosis Without Action
paths.outcome: Rachel's community cardiologist acknowledges the BAG3 result but does not change her management, refer her to cardiogenetics, or mention gene therapy trials. She does not discover the DCM Foundation or BAG3 communities until months later when her disease progresses. Her children are not tested — partly because Rachel is not counseled on the urgency, and partly because the psychosocial weight of testing young children is too heavy without professional guidance. The genetic information sits in a report in her chart. It does not change her care trajectory.
paths.key_driver: Community cardiologist lacks BAG3-specific knowledge; no systematic pathway from genetic result to trial awareness or registry enrollment; absence of proactive genetic counseling delays cascade screening.

journey.narrative_description: With BAG3 confirmed as autosomal dominant, Rachel and her husband face the question they have been dreading since the genetics appointment: whether — and when — to test their children, ages 8 and 5, for the family variant. Their genetic counselor walks them through the framework carefully: both children's echocardiograms come back normal, which is expected at their ages. The variant's penetrance is age-dependent; cardiac manifestation in childhood is rare. But the genetic information, if positive, changes how Rachel thinks about every cough, every episode of fatigue, every athletic exertion. After weeks of deliberation and three sessions with a pediatric genetic counselor, they decide to defer predictive genetic testing until each child is old enough to participate in the decision — likely adolescence. The children's echocardiograms will be repeated every two to three years as cardiac surveillance. It is the most carefully considered decision Rachel has made. It does not make her feel better.
journey.quote: We decided not to test them. Not yet. We wanted them to have a childhood that wasn't organized around a result we couldn't change. But I watch them run across the playground and I'm doing math in my head. Fifty percent. Fifty percent.

events.label: Both Children's Echocardiograms Normal — Surveillance Plan Established
events.description: Echocardiograms for both children (ages 8 and 5) return normal. A cardiac surveillance schedule is established — repeat echocardiograms every 2-3 years through adolescence. The normal results provide temporary relief without resolving the underlying genetic uncertainty.
events.tooltip: Both children's echos normal at current ages. Surveillance echocardiograms scheduled every 2-3 years. Genetic status remains unknown by parental decision.

events.label: Predictive Genetic Testing Deferred — Psychosocial Weight of Early Disclosure
events.description: After deliberation with a pediatric genetic counselor, Rachel and her husband defer predictive genetic testing for their children. The decision reflects both the absence of pediatric-onset disease in BAG3 and the psychosocial burden of disclosing a variant result to a child who cannot yet consent to knowing. The deferral is clinically appropriate but emotionally incomplete — it resolves the immediate decision without resolving the underlying anxiety.
events.tooltip: Predictive genetic testing deferred pending children's ability to participate in consent. Clinically appropriate; emotionally unresolved. Annual surveillance established as interim framework.

sentiment_reasons: The decision to defer testing is carefully made but not emotionally relieving — the uncertainty is the same, only the information is withheld by choice
Normal pediatric echocardiograms provide genuine but temporary reassurance; Rachel knows the variant's penetrance is age-dependent
The permanence of the surveillance obligation — repeat echoes every few years, indefinitely — makes the genetic inheritance tangible and ongoing

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Sustained low-grade anxiety punctuated by the temporary relief of normal pediatric echos — the emotional labor of the deferral decision is real and ongoing.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in the pediatric genetic counselor is high; the process of deliberate, counseled decision-making partially restores the sense of agency that the diagnosis had eroded.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel understands the clinical rationale for deferral and the surveillance protocol — her health literacy allows her to participate meaningfully in the decision.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Pediatric cardiology appointments, genetic counseling sessions, and the ongoing emotional labor of the deferral add to the cumulative burden of managing a genetic cardiac condition.

journey.narrative_description: Rachel focuses her analysis on the three BAG3 gene therapy programs. She creates a detailed comparison: Rocket's RP-A701 (Phase 1, AAVrh.74 capsid, sites at UCSD/Mayo/MUSC, ~8 patients, estimated start June 2026); Alexion's ALXN2350/DCMRestore (Phase 1/2, AAV9 capsid, already recruiting since October 2025); and Affinia's AFTX-201/UPBEAT (Phase 1/2, proprietary cardiotropic capsid, IND cleared February 2026, claims 5-10x lower dosing). She maps her own eligibility against each trial's criteria — her LVEF of 34% and NYHA Class II status place her in the eligible window for all three. Her ICD was implanted 10 months ago, clearing the 3-month seasoning requirement. She has never participated in a gene therapy study. On paper, she qualifies. The question is which trial to pursue — and whether choosing one permanently forecloses the others. All three trials require stable GDMT for at least 30 days, so Rachel's medication regimen is frozen during this evaluation period.
journey.quote: I made a spreadsheet. Three trials, three different capsids, three different companies. All of them want my specific mutation. I'm the exact patient they designed these for. But if I pick one and it doesn't work, the other two doors close forever.

events.label: Rachel Confirms Eligibility Across All Three Trials
events.description: Rachel maps her clinical profile against each trial's published criteria: LVEF 34% (within 25-45% window for RP-A701; within range for ALXN2350 and AFTX-201), NYHA Class II, ICD implanted 10 months ago, stable GDMT for 30+ days, no prior gene therapy, confirmed BAG3 pathogenic variant. She appears eligible for all three. This is rare — the Venn diagram of eligibility across three simultaneous trials in a population this small means she is genuinely valuable to all three sponsors.
events.tooltip: Rachel confirms eligibility for all three BAG3 gene therapy trials — RP-A701, ALXN2350, AFTX-201. Her clinical profile maps cleanly to all published criteria. She is a high-value candidate for all three sponsors.

events.label: Prior Gene Therapy Exclusion — Choosing One Trial Forecloses the Others
events.description: All three trials exclude patients with prior gene therapy exposure. Enrolling in any one permanently disqualifies Rachel from the other two. With three different capsids (AAVrh.74, AAV9, proprietary cardiotropic), three different dosing strategies, and no Phase 1 efficacy data yet available for any of them, Rachel is being asked to make an irreversible decision with radically incomplete information.
events.tooltip: All three trials exclude prior gene therapy. Enrolling in one permanently forecloses the other two. Three different capsids, no efficacy data — irreversible decision under radical uncertainty.

events.label: Geographic Burden — Nearest Sites Are 4.5+ Hours Away
events.description: The nearest RP-A701 trial sites (UCSD, MUSC) are 4.5+ hours from Rachel's home. Alexion's sites are not yet fully disclosed. Affinia's UPBEAT locations are pending site initiation. Each visit requires her husband to take time off work and her mother to manage childcare. Multi-day visits for infusion and monitoring would amplify this burden significantly.
