events.tooltip: Nearest trial sites 4.5+ hours away. Each visit requires husband's time off work + mother's childcare coverage. Multi-day infusion visits would amplify burden.

events.label: BAG3 Gene Therapy Trial Comparison Tool — Sponsor-Neutral, Patient-Facing
events.description: A sponsor-neutral, patient-facing digital tool — maintained by the DCM Foundation or an independent party — that presents all active BAG3 gene therapy trials in a structured comparison format (capsid type, dosing strategy, trial phase, visit cadence, site locations, eligibility criteria, and the implications of the prior gene therapy exclusion) would address the specific information architecture problem Rachel faces. She built her own spreadsheet; most patients cannot. A curated tool would democratize the comparison and reduce the cognitive load of a decision that carries irreversible consequences.
events.tooltip: Sponsor-neutral BAG3 gene therapy trial comparison tool — structured, patient-facing, addresses the irreversibility and information asymmetry of the one-shot trial decision.
events.partner: DCM Foundation / Genetic Cardiomyopathy Awareness Consortium

events.label: Travel and Logistical Support Program for Trial Participants
events.description: A sponsor-funded travel and logistical support program — covering travel costs, accommodation near trial sites, and childcare coordination assistance for participants with young children — would directly address Rachel's primary logistical barrier. The geographic concentration of BAG3 expertise at a handful of academic centers means that most eligible patients will face significant travel burden. Converting that barrier from terry's problem to the sponsor's investment is a recruitment accelerant.
events.tooltip: Sponsor-funded travel, accommodation, and childcare support for trial participants — converts geographic barrier from patient's problem to sponsor's investment in recruitment.

sentiment_reasons: Confirming eligibility across all three trials is genuinely empowering — Rachel is not just a candidate but a high-value one
The irreversibility of the prior-gene-therapy exclusion transforms a choice between options into a permanent foreclosure of alternatives
The geographic burden is solvable in principle but requires sustained logistical support that Rachel cannot unilaterally provide

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Cautious optimism — genuine options exist for the first time, but the irreversibility of the choice and the information gaps prevent clean positive sentiment.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in the research ecosystem is growing; Rachel is developing relationships with trial site coordinators through initial outreach. Her community cardiologist remains uninvolved.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel's comparison spreadsheet and her ability to map her clinical profile against three sets of eligibility criteria represent peak self-directed health literacy.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Geographic distance, childcare coordination, and her husband's work constraints create a structural logistical ceiling that motivation alone cannot overcome.

journey.narrative_description: Three converging pressures bring Rachel to a decision point. Her latest echocardiogram shows stable LVEF at 34% — she is not getting worse, but she is not getting better, and BAG3-DCM natural history data suggests progressive decline is the expected trajectory. Alexion's DCMRestore trial has been recruiting since October 2025 and may fill its early cohorts before Rachel completes screening. Rocket's RP-A701 trial targets June 2026 enrollment, giving her a narrow window. And her 8-year-old daughter, who knows her mother 'has a heart problem,' asks one evening whether the problem will ever go away. Rachel tells her yes. Then she sits at the kitchen table with her husband and her spreadsheet and confronts the question she has been circling for weeks: which trial, and when. Any medication modification at this stage would restart the 30-day GDMT stability window, so her regimen remains frozen.
journey.quote: My daughter asked me if my heart problem will ever go away. I told her yes. And then I had to decide what I was actually going to do to make that true — not just for her question, but for the next thirty years of answers.

events.label: Enrollment Windows Narrowing — Competing Timelines Compress Decision
events.description: ALXN2350 has been recruiting since October 2025 and early dose cohorts may be filling. RP-A701 targets June 2026 enrollment start with only ~8 patients total. AFTX-201's timeline is less defined. The extremely small enrollment targets (8-20 patients across all three trials) mean that delays in decision-making carry real opportunity costs. Rachel is acutely aware that the window is not indefinite.
events.tooltip: All three trials combined seek fewer than 30 patients from a global BAG3-DCM population. Enrollment windows are narrow and dose cohorts may fill. Decision delay carries real opportunity cost.

events.label: Phase 1 Risk Profile — First-in-Human Gene Therapy in a Patient Who Is 'Stable'
events.description: Rachel's current clinical status — stable on GDMT, LVEF 34%, NYHA Class II, functioning as a teacher and parent — means she is enrolling in a first-in-human gene therapy from a position of relative stability. The risk-benefit calculus is different from a patient with LVEF 25% and NYHA Class III. She is not desperate enough for the risk to feel obvious, but she is informed enough to know that BAG3-DCM's natural trajectory makes early intervention more rational than waiting for deterioration.
events.tooltip: Risk-benefit tension: stable enough that Phase 1 gene therapy risk is non-trivial; informed enough to know BAG3 natural history favors early intervention. Neither panic nor complacency — informed anxiety.

events.label: Family Decision Session — Kitchen Table with the Spreadsheet
events.description: Rachel and her husband set aside an evening after the children are asleep to review the three options systematically — with Rachel's comparison spreadsheet, the Vienna-style approach of deliberate shared decision-making. They weigh capsid types, dosing strategies, site logistics, and the irreversibility of the prior gene therapy exclusion. They also discuss what to tell Rachel's mother, who will need to relocate temporarily to help with childcare during trial visits.
events.tooltip: Couple schedules structured decision session with comparison spreadsheet — shared deliberation as reclaimed agency in a decision that affects the entire family.

events.label: Pre-Enrollment Genetic Counseling for Gene Therapy — Risk, Irreversibility, and Family Planning
events.description: A dedicated pre-enrollment genetic counseling session — specifically designed for BAG3-DCM patients considering gene therapy trials — would address the unique dimensions of Rachel's decision: AAV capsid immunogenicity and re-dosing limitations, the prior gene therapy exclusion across competing trials, the distinction between gene replacement and gene editing, and the implications for her children's future eligibility for gene-based therapies. Standard genetic counseling covers inheritance; this session covers the therapeutic frontier.
events.tooltip: Dedicated gene therapy decision counseling for BAG3 patients — addresses capsid immunogenicity, re-dosing limitations, cross-trial exclusion, and next-generation therapy implications.
events.partner: Trial sites / National Society of Genetic Counselors

events.label: No Mechanism to Compare Trials Across Competing Sponsors
events.description: At her most consequential decision point, Rachel has no sponsor-neutral resource that objectively compares the three BAG3 gene therapy programs. ClinicalTrials.gov provides raw protocol data but not comparative framing. Each sponsor's materials present only their own trial. The DCM Foundation does not yet offer a structured comparison tool. Rachel built her own spreadsheet — a remarkable feat of patient agency that most BAG3 patients could not replicate. The absence of a curated comparison resource means that trial selection is determined by information access and analytical capacity rather than clinical fit.
events.tooltip: Missing resource: no sponsor-neutral comparison of competing BAG3 gene therapy trials — trial selection currently determined by patient's individual research capacity rather than structured decision support.

inflection_detail.label: Gene Therapy Trial Selection — Irreversible One-Shot Decision
inflection_detail.description: Rachel reaches a convergence of clinical trajectory, enrollment window pressure, and family emotional weight that forces an irreversible decision without adequate information. Each trial offers a different capsid, dosing strategy, and risk profile — but no efficacy data exists for any of them. Choosing one permanently excludes the others. The structural feature driving this decision's difficulty is not Rachel's clinical profile (which maps cleanly to all three trials) but the competitive landscape among sponsors — three companies racing to recruit from a patient population smaller than a single high school class. Rachel's decision quality is determined by her exceptional analytical capacity and self-directed research. Most BAG3 patients — those without her education, health literacy, or internet access — would face this decision with far less information and support.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Establish a BAG3-specific patient navigation and decision support program — providing pre-enrollment counseling, structured trial comparison, travel logistics support, and a dedicated patient navigator who can serve as a single point of contact across the screening-to-enrollment journey. The program should be sponsor-funded but navigation-neutral, recognizing that every eligible patient who enrolls in any BAG3 gene therapy trial advances the field — regardless of which sponsor's product they receive.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Number of confirmed BAG3 pathogenic variant carriers who are aware of gene therapy trials; conversion rate from trial awareness to screening contact; reasons for non-enrollment among eligible patients; average time from BAG3 confirmation to trial enrollment decision; role of geographic distance, childcare burden, and cross-trial exclusion anxiety in enrollment decisions.

paths.label: Informed Enrollment — Structured Decision with Family Alignment
paths.outcome: Rachel and her husband select the trial whose site logistics and visit cadence are most compatible with their family constraints — prioritizing the site that offers travel reimbursement and has a coordinator who can accommodate her school schedule. They accept the irreversibility of the prior gene therapy exclusion as the cost of acting on the best available information now rather than waiting for certainty that will not arrive. Rachel's mother agrees to relocate temporarily during the infusion and monitoring period. The family frames the decision as the most active, informed thing they can do together — not a gamble, but a deliberate investment in a future where Rachel is present for her daughter's milestones.
paths.key_driver: Rachel's analytical comparison of all three trials; family alignment on the logistics plan; trial site travel reimbursement and scheduling flexibility; acceptance that acting on partial information is preferable to waiting for complete information that may never arrive.

paths.label: Decision Paralysis — Window Closes While Waiting for Clarity
paths.outcome: Unable to resolve the cross-trial exclusion anxiety and lacking a trusted advisor who can help her weigh the options, Rachel defers the decision. She tells herself she will 'wait for more data' from the first cohorts. Six months later, ALXN2350's early dose cohorts are full, RP-A701's enrollment has begun without her, and her next echo shows an LVEF of 30% — below the lower bound of some trial criteria. She eventually enrolls in whichever trial still has slots, under more clinical urgency and less decision quality than would have been possible six months earlier. The delay cost her options, not time.
paths.key_driver: Cross-trial exclusion anxiety unresolved; no pre-enrollment counseling or decision support; absence of trusted medical advisor who understands the gene therapy landscape; assumption that waiting produces better information when in fact it produces fewer options.

sentiment_reasons: Enrollment windows for trials enrolling 8-20 patients from a global rare population are genuinely narrow — delay is not neutral
The daughter's question is not symbolic — it is the emotional truth of what is at stake, articulated without any awareness of its weight
Scheduling a deliberate family decision session represents Rachel's most characteristic act of agency — converting a crisis into a structured choice

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The convergence of enrollment pressure, risk-benefit tension, and family emotional weight produces a state of loaded, active uncertainty — informed anxiety rather than despair or optimism.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in the research ecosystem (trial sites, coordinators, Vienna-style specialists) is adequate; the absence of a trusted local medical advisor who understands gene therapy is the gap.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel's comparison spreadsheet and structured family decision session demonstrate that she has converted distress into deliberate agency — the hallmark of high self-efficacy under duress.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Geographic distance, childcare coordination, husband's work constraints, and mother's relocation logistics create a structural ceiling. The family can manage one trial's visit cadence; they cannot manage the uncertainty of not knowing which trial to commit to.

treatment_changes.note: Protocol-specified prophylaxis for AAV capsid tolerance — first non-HF medication in Rachel's regimen. GDMT held stable.

journey.narrative_description: Rachel has selected her trial and made initial contact with the site coordinator. She enters the formal screening period — a multi-visit process involving repeat echocardiography, cardiac MRI, blood work including AAV neutralizing antibody titers, a six-minute walk test, and a comprehensive medication review. Her GDMT remains frozen throughout. The trial protocol introduces her first non-cardiac medication: a corticosteroid taper prescribed as immunosuppressive prophylaxis to promote AAV capsid tolerance at the time of infusion. The screening process is intensive and logistically demanding — two visits to the trial site over three weeks, each requiring overnight travel. Her mother has relocated temporarily. Her students know she has 'appointments.' The trial coordinator is the most informed, responsive medical contact Rachel has encountered in four years of navigating this disease. She arrives at each visit prepared, organized, and quietly terrified.
journey.quote: The screening process is the most medically thorough thing I've ever experienced. They know my mutation. They know my echo. They know what they're looking for. For the first time, I feel like the system was built for someone like me.

events.label: Formal Screening Initiated — Multi-Visit Evaluation at Trial Site
events.description: Rachel begins the formal screening process: repeat echo, cardiac MRI, comprehensive labs including AAV neutralizing antibody titers, 6-minute walk test, and medication review. Two site visits over three weeks, each requiring overnight travel. The process is the most thorough medical evaluation she has received since her initial hospitalization.
events.tooltip: Formal trial screening initiated — repeat echo, cardiac MRI, AAV NAb titers, 6MWT. Two site visits over three weeks. Most comprehensive evaluation since initial hospitalization.

events.label: Screening Logistics — Overnight Travel, Childcare, Work Absence
events.description: Two overnight trips to the trial site in three weeks require: Rachel's mother relocating temporarily, her husband taking partial leave, and Rachel preparing substitute lesson plans for missed teaching days. The logistical burden is manageable because the family has pre-planned it — but it reveals how demanding the full trial participation will be.
events.tooltip: Two overnight site visits in three weeks — mother relocated, husband on partial leave, sub plans prepared. Previews the logistical demands of full trial participation.

events.label: Trial Coordinator Becomes Primary Medical Contact — Highest-Quality HCP Relationship
events.description: The trial site coordinator — who has specific BAG3 expertise and a detailed knowledge of Rachel's clinical profile — becomes the most responsive and informed medical contact Rachel has encountered in four years. This relationship partially fills the gap left by her community cardiologist's inability to engage with her genotype-specific disease.
events.tooltip: Trial coordinator with BAG3-specific expertise becomes Rachel's highest-quality HCP relationship — more genotype-informed than any provider she has previously encountered.

sentiment_reasons: Entering formal screening is the most concrete action Rachel has taken toward a disease-modifying intervention — it converts aspiration into process
The trial coordinator's BAG3-specific knowledge provides a quality of medical engagement Rachel has not experienced in four years
The logistical burden of screening previews the sustained demands of trial participation — manageable but not invisible

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Cautious optimism with persistent background anxiety — Rachel is the furthest she has ever been toward a potential disease-modifying treatment, and the furthest she has ever been from turning back.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust peaks — the trial coordinator's genotype-specific expertise provides a quality of engagement that exceeds anything Rachel has experienced in standard care.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Rachel arrives at each screening visit fully prepared — organized records, informed questions, a clear understanding of what is being assessed and why.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Pre-planned logistics allow the family to manage screening demands, but the structural constraints (travel, childcare, work) are at their operational limit.

personas.indication: BAG3-Associated Dilated Cardiomyopathy with Ventricular Arrhythmia

profile.name: Aaron M.
current_treatments: Standard heart failure medications (ACE inhibitor or ARNi, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i — regimen ongoing)
past_treatments: Initial HF medications prior to arrhythmia stabilization
profile.preference: Seeks stability and understanding of his dual-diagnosis complexity; values clear communication from specialists
profile.diagnosed: Diagnosed at age 41 with PBC, atrial fibrillation, heart failure, and subsequently BAG3-DCM; VT episode approximately one year post-diagnosis
profile.quote: I went in feeling weak and came out with four diagnoses. Then a year later my heart tried to kill me and a machine in my chest saved my life. I'm 'OK' now. Whatever that means.
profile.bio_1: Aaron was 41 when general weakness prompted him to see a doctor — a decision he had been putting off, attributing the fatigue to the demands of everyday life. What followed was a diagnostic cascade: primary biliary cholangitis, atrial fibrillation, and then, during a cardiology workup, a discovery that reframed everything. He was in heart failure. An EP study revealed a potentially lethal ventricular arrhythmia, and an ICD was implanted the following day. Genetic testing later confirmed a BAG3 pathogenic variant — meaning the cardiac disease was almost certainly not incidental but written into his biology from birth.
profile.bio_2: Aaron manages the complexity of a dual-system disease: a slowly progressing autoimmune liver condition running in parallel with a genetically driven cardiomyopathy prone to dangerous arrhythmias. Approximately one year after his initial diagnosis, he experienced his first episode of ventricular tachycardia — an event he describes as the single most terrifying experience of his life. The ICD fired. He survived. A 10-day hospitalization followed, focused on antiarrhythmic medication escalation. He now lives with regular but mostly mild VT episodes, intermittent general weakness, and the ever-present awareness that his ICD is not a cure but a last line of defense. He describes himself as 'OK' — a word that carries the weight of everything he has adapted to.
profile.goal1: Achieve durable arrhythmia control that reduces VT episodes and eliminates the fear of ICD shocks.
profile.goal2: Understand the relationship between his BAG3-DCM and his PBC — and whether either condition is accelerating the other.
profile.goal3: Identify whether gene therapy trials or other disease-modifying options are relevant to his specific clinical profile.v
profile.barrier1: Complexity of managing two serious conditions simultaneously — PBC and BAG3-DCM — each requiring specialist care that does not always communicate across specialties.
profile.barrier2: Psychological burden of living with a device and recurrent VT episodes — the knowledge that his heart has already tried to kill him once shapes every day.
profile.barrier3: Uncertainty about whether his current antiarrhythmic regimen (amiodarone/mexiletine) is sustainable long-term given amiodarone's known organ toxicity — particularly in a patient with existing liver disease.

discussionThemes.label: Daily functioning and quality of life

journey.narrative_description: Aaron is 41 when persistent general weakness prompts him to see a doctor. He is not in crisis — the fatigue is something he has been rationalizing and deferring. His initial visit triggers a broader workup. Within a compressed diagnostic window, he receives two diagnoses he was not expecting: primary biliary cholangitis, a chronic autoimmune liver condition causing slow deterioration of the bile ducts, and atrial fibrillation. The liver diagnosis carries a manageable prognosis with appropriate medication. The AFib triggers an urgent cardiology referral. Aaron processes both diagnoses simultaneously, with no prior framework for either.
journey.quote: I went in feeling run down. I figured I needed to sleep more or eat better. I came out with two diagnoses I'd never heard of and a cardiology appointment I wasn't expecting.

events.label: PBC Diagnosed — Manageable with UDCA
events.description: Primary biliary cholangitis identified. Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) initiated to slow bile duct deterioration. Prognosis with treatment is relatively favorable — PBC is a chronic but manageable condition in most patients. Aaron receives the diagnosis as serious but not immediately threatening.
events.tooltip: PBC diagnosed and treated with UDCA. Chronic but manageable with appropriate therapy. Aaron's first encounter with serious medical diagnosis.

events.label: Atrial Fibrillation Discovered — Cardiac Evaluation Triggered
events.description: Atrial fibrillation detected during initial workup. Cardiology referral initiated urgently. The AFib finding reframes Aaron's general weakness from a lifestyle issue to a potentially cardiac-origin symptom. He enters the cardiology system for the first time.
events.tooltip: AFib detected during initial workup — urgent cardiology referral initiated. General weakness now recontextualized as potentially cardiac in origin.

journey.step: Cardiology Workup — Heart Failure and Arrhythmia Identified

treatment_changes.drug: Standard HF regimen (ACE inhibitor / beta-blocker / MRA)
treatment_changes.note: First formal HF medications initiated following heart failure diagnosis
Continued from PBC diagnosis

journey.narrative_description: The cardiology evaluation moves quickly. An echocardiogram reveals reduced ejection fraction consistent with heart failure — a finding that recontextualizes every symptom Aaron had attributed to aging or stress. His cardiologist recommends an electrophysiology study to characterize the arrhythmia burden given his AFib and depressed cardiac function. The EP study identifies a potentially lethal ventricular arrhythmia. The decision to implant an ICD is made the same day. Aaron goes from a man who went to the doctor feeling tired to a heart failure patient with a device scheduled for the next morning.
journey.quote: They did the study and told me the same day that my heart had a dangerous rhythm problem and I needed a device implanted tomorrow. There was no transition period. It just happened.

events.label: Heart Failure Diagnosed — Reduced Ejection Fraction on Echo
events.description: Echocardiogram reveals reduced ejection fraction. Heart failure diagnosis established. Standard HF medications initiated. Aaron's general weakness now has a structural cardiac explanation — but the explanation introduces a new, much larger clinical reality than he entered the medical system to address.
events.tooltip: Reduced EF on echocardiogram confirms heart failure. First HF medications initiated. General weakness retroactively explained — and significantly reframed.

events.label: EP Study Identifies Lethal Arrhythmia — ICD Decision Made Same Day
events.description: Electrophysiology study characterizes a potentially lethal ventricular arrhythmia in the context of AFib and depressed EF. The EP team makes the ICD recommendation the same day. Aaron's cardiologist explains that the device could save his life. Aaron has no prior framework for any of this; the pace of the decisions leaves limited room for processing.
events.tooltip: EP study reveals potentially lethal ventricular arrhythmia. ICD indicated and scheduled for next day — decision made within hours of EP study completion.

treatment_changes.note: Implanted for primary/secondary arrhythmia prevention following EP study findings

journey.narrative_description: The ICD is implanted the day after the EP study. The procedure is completed without complication. Aaron leaves the hospital with a device in his chest that is, simultaneously, the most reassuring and most terrifying thing he has ever experienced. The ICD means the medical team takes his arrhythmia risk seriously enough to act on it immediately. It also means Aaron now wears the physical evidence of a life-threatening condition every day. He is started on standard heart failure medications and discharged with a follow-up plan. He does not yet know his disease has a genetic cause.
journey.quote: I woke up with a lump under my collarbone and a new understanding of what my life was now. The doctor said it could save my life. I believed him. I also didn't know how to feel about needing it.

events.label: ICD Implanted — Procedure Uncomplicated
events.description: Single-chamber or dual-chamber ICD implanted the day following EP study. Procedure completed without complication. Aaron is discharged on standard HF medications with cardiology and EP follow-up scheduled. The device provides immediate arrhythmia protection but introduces ongoing psychological weight — device awareness, activity restrictions, and the constant reminder of cardiac vulnerability.
events.tooltip: ICD implanted day after EP study — uncomplicated. Discharged on HF medications. Device provides protection while introducing persistent illness identity.

events.label: Dual-Diagnosis Complexity — PBC and HF Requiring Separate Specialist Tracks
events.description: Aaron now has active management needs across hepatology (PBC/UDCA), cardiology (HF medications), and electrophysiology (ICD management and arrhythmia monitoring). These specialties rarely communicate directly. Aaron becomes the de facto coordinator of his own multi-system care — translating between providers who may not be aware of each other's management decisions.
events.tooltip: Three active specialist tracks — hepatology, cardiology, electrophysiology — with no natural care coordination mechanism. Aaron manages the interfaces himself.

journey.narrative_description: Genetic testing ordered as part of the workup for non-ischemic heart failure returns a pathogenic BAG3 variant. The finding reclassifies Aaron's DCM from idiopathic to genetically determined — explaining both the structural disease and the arrhythmia burden. The genetic result is simultaneously clarifying and permanent. It means Aaron's cardiomyopathy is not an accident of aging or lifestyle — it was encoded before he was born. It also raises questions about family members who may carry the same variant. Aaron's response is characteristically analytical: he wants to understand what the diagnosis means, what it changes, and what the options are.
journey.quote: Finding out it was genetic was almost a relief. At least there was a reason. But then I started thinking about what else it means — for me, for my family, for how this is going to go.

events.label: BAG3 Pathogenic Variant Confirmed — DCM Reclassified
events.description: Genetic panel returns positive for a pathogenic BAG3 variant. Aaron's cardiomyopathy is reclassified from idiopathic to BAG3-associated DCM — one of the most arrhythmia-prone subtypes of genetic cardiomyopathy. The finding explains both his structural disease and his high arrhythmia burden. His cardiologist confirms the diagnosis is hereditary and discusses cascade testing for family members.
events.tooltip: BAG3 pathogenic variant confirmed. DCM reclassified — explains both structural disease and arrhythmia burden. Autosomal dominant — family cascade testing recommended.

events.label: BAG3 Cardiomyopathy Patient Navigation — Trial and Registry Awareness at Diagnosis
events.description: A sponsor-supported navigation pathway triggered at BAG3 confirmation — providing curated information about natural history registries, gene therapy trial eligibility, and BAG3-specific patient communities — would intercept Aaron at the moment of highest information-seeking motivation. At the time of his genetic diagnosis, Aaron does not yet know that three AAV gene therapy programs targeting BAG3 are in active development. Without a structured pathway, that knowledge depends on his own research capacity.
events.tooltip: BAG3-triggered navigation pathway — trial awareness, registry enrollment, and patient community connection at the moment of genetic diagnosis.
events.partner: DCM Foundation / Clinical genetic testing labs

events.label: Limited BAG3-Specific Guidance from Treating Cardiologist
events.description: Aaron's cardiologist confirms the BAG3 finding and discusses inheritance, but cannot provide BAG3-specific prognostic nuance, management implications beyond current HF therapy, or awareness of active gene therapy trials. The result is documented but does not substantively change Aaron's care plan.
events.tooltip: Cardiologist confirms BAG3 but cannot contextualize the genotype — no trial awareness, no management changes, no BAG3-specific prognostic guidance.

journey.narrative_description: Approximately one year after his initial diagnosis, Aaron experiences his first episode of ventricular tachycardia. The ICD fires. He describes the event as the single most terrifying experience of his life — a characterization he offers without qualification or hedging. The shock is sudden, physical, and total. There is no preparation for the sensation of a device delivering a defibrillation shock, and no psychological framework adequate to the moment. He is transported to the hospital. The immediate clinical picture is serious: sustained VT in a patient with BAG3-DCM, reduced EF, and a device that has already had to intervene once. The 10-day hospitalization that follows is focused on preventing the next episode.
journey.quote: The ICD fired. I don't have words for what that felt like. It was the most terrifying thing that has ever happened to me. It was also the thing that kept me alive.

events.label: Sustained VT Episode — ICD Shock Delivered
events.description: First episode of sustained ventricular tachycardia approximately one year post-diagnosis. ICD detects and terminates the arrhythmia with a defibrillation shock. Aaron survives. The event is the first actualization of the risk the EP team identified when they implanted the device — and the first time the abstract possibility of sudden cardiac arrest becomes a concrete lived experience.
events.tooltip: First sustained VT episode — ICD fires and terminates arrhythmia. Abstract arrhythmia risk becomes concrete lived experience. Most acute event in Aaron's journey to date.

events.label: ICD Performs as Designed — Life-Saving Defibrillation Successful
events.description: The ICD terminates the VT episode successfully. From a purely technical standpoint, the device did exactly what it was implanted to do. This is not comfort in the immediate aftermath, but it is the clinical fact that Aaron carries forward: the system worked. He is alive because a decision made a year ago was correct.
events.tooltip: ICD terminates VT successfully — device performed as designed. Life-saving function confirmed. Technical success that does not immediately translate to emotional relief.

treatment_changes.note: Antiarrhythmic escalation following sustained VT episode — loaded and initiated during hospitalization
Added in combination with amiodarone for VT suppression

journey.narrative_description: Aaron spends 10 days in the hospital. The focus is antiarrhythmic medication escalation: amiodarone is loaded and initiated, mexiletine added for additional VT suppression. The combination is among the most aggressive antiarrhythmic regimens available in standard practice. The team monitors Aaron for drug tolerance, proarrhythmic effects, and hemodynamic stability. He is discharged when the arrhythmia burden appears controlled. There is a conversation — not long enough, Aaron feels — about what comes next. He leaves the hospital different from how he arrived.
journey.quote: Ten days. They loaded me up on amiodarone and mexiletine and monitored everything. I left feeling like the medications were keeping a lid on something that was still there.

events.label: Amiodarone + Mexiletine Initiated — VT Burden Suppressed
events.description: Amiodarone loaded and initiated; mexiletine added for combination antiarrhythmic therapy. The regimen represents a significant escalation — both drugs carry meaningful side effect profiles and require careful monitoring. Arrhythmia burden decreases during hospitalization. Aaron is discharged on the combination.
events.tooltip: Amiodarone + mexiletine combination initiated during hospitalization. Aggressive antiarrhythmic strategy reflects the severity of Aaron's VT burden — effective for suppression, demanding in terms of monitoring.

events.label: Amiodarone Organ Toxicity Risk — Compounded by Pre-Existing PBC
events.description: Amiodarone carries well-documented risks of hepatotoxicity, pulmonary toxicity, and thyroid dysfunction with long-term use. In a patient with pre-existing primary biliary cholangitis — a chronic liver condition already under treatment — the hepatotoxicity profile of amiodarone creates a surveillance requirement that neither specialty has a clean protocol for. The two conditions are being managed independently, but the drug that treats one creates specific risk for the other.
events.tooltip: Amiodarone hepatotoxicity risk in a patient with pre-existing PBC — two conditions creating a compounded monitoring requirement that crosses specialty lines.

events.label: Cross-Specialty Pharmacovigilance Protocol — Amiodarone Monitoring in PBC
events.description: A structured cross-specialty monitoring protocol — co-developed by Aaron's hepatology and electrophysiology teams — specifying shared liver function monitoring intervals, amiodarone dose review triggers, and a pre-agreed escalation pathway if hepatic signals emerge, would address the most clinically dangerous gap in Aaron's current care. Without it, the two teams make independent decisions about a drug that sits at the interface of both conditions.
events.tooltip: Structured hepatology-electrophysiology co-monitoring for amiodarone in PBC — addresses the cross-specialty pharmacovigilance gap created by dual-condition complexity.

journey.step: Living with Recurrent VT — Current State
journey.narrative_description: Aaron's current daily reality is defined by a qualified equilibrium he describes as 'so-so.' Ventricular tachycardia episodes continue — mostly mild, managed by the medication regimen — but their recurrence means the threat is never fully resolved, only suppressed. He experiences intermittent general weakness. He is, by any clinical measure, doing reasonably well for a man with a weakened heart on a dual antiarrhythmic regimen. He is also acutely aware that 'doing well' in his context is a relative term. He monitors his body for signals the way someone monitors weather — not in panic, but in constant, low-grade readiness. He is 43 years old.
journey.quote: Fairly regular VT episodes but mostly mild. Some general weakness. Otherwise OK — for a guy with a weakened heart. That's where I am.

events.label: Recurrent VT on Dual Antiarrhythmic Therapy — Suppressed but Not Resolved
events.description: VT episodes continue despite amiodarone and mexiletine. Most are mild and not shock-requiring, suggesting the regimen is providing meaningful suppression but not elimination. Each episode is a reminder that the underlying arrhythmia substrate — the genetically damaged myocardium — remains. Aaron navigates the distinction between 'controlled' and 'cured' every day.
events.tooltip: Recurrent VT episodes on dual antiarrhythmic therapy — mostly mild but ongoing. Medication suppresses the arrhythmia without eliminating it. The substrate remains.

events.label: Long-Term Amiodarone Toxicity Horizon — Sustainable Regimen Uncertainty
events.description: Amiodarone's cumulative toxicity profile — particularly hepatic and pulmonary — means that the current antiarrhythmic strategy may not be sustainable indefinitely. Aaron is 43. If his VT burden requires amiodarone for a decade or more, the cumulative risk of organ damage competes with the benefit of arrhythmia suppression. In the context of his PBC, this calculation is more compressed than it would be in a patient with healthy baseline liver function.
events.tooltip: Amiodarone cumulative toxicity horizon — not immediately urgent, but structurally concerning for a 43-year-old with pre-existing liver disease facing long-term antiarrhythmic therapy.

events.label: BAG3 Gene Therapy Trial Evaluation — Disease-Modifying Option Awareness
events.description: Aaron's current management treats the symptoms of BAG3-DCM — arrhythmia suppression, HF optimization — without addressing the genetic root cause. Three AAV gene therapy programs (Rocket's RP-A701, Alexion's ALXN2350, Affinia's AFTX-201) are in active clinical development for BAG3-DCM. Whether Aaron's current clinical profile — arrhythmia burden, antiarrhythmic medications, PBC — would affect his eligibility requires specialist evaluation. Awareness of these options has not yet been systematically surfaced to him through his care team.
events.tooltip: Three BAG3 gene therapy trials in active development — disease-modifying options Aaron may not yet be aware of. Specialist evaluation needed to assess eligibility given antiarrhythmic regimen and PBC.

inflection_detail.label: Current Equilibrium — Managed but Not Modified
inflection_detail.description: Aaron has reached a functional but fragile equilibrium: VT suppressed on dual antiarrhythmic therapy, HF managed on standard GDMT, PBC controlled on UDCA. Nothing is deteriorating acutely. Nothing is improving fundamentally. The BAG3 variant that caused his disease remains active in every cardiac myocyte, slowly continuing the protein quality-control failure that will, without disease-modifying intervention, drive progressive structural decline. The question Aaron's current clinical situation raises is not whether he needs more of the same — it is whether the system will surface the emerging disease-modifying options before his window for intervention narrows.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Aaron is an engaged, analytically capable patient who has navigated extraordinary diagnostic complexity. He is not passive — but he is dependent on his care team to surface options that cross specialty lines. A BAG3-specific patient navigator, connected to his cardiology and hepatology teams, could evaluate his gene therapy trial eligibility and help him weigh the implications of his PBC and antiarrhythmic regimen for trial enrollment. The additional complexity of his dual diagnosis makes specialist navigation more important, not less.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Eligibility criteria for active BAG3 gene therapy trials with respect to antiarrhythmic medications (amiodarone, mexiletine); impact of pre-existing liver disease on AAV gene therapy protocols; whether ICD implantation timeline affects trial eligibility; natural history trajectory for BAG3-DCM patients with high arrhythmia burden on dual antiarrhythmic therapy.

paths.label: Disease-Modifying Pathway — Gene Therapy Evaluation
paths.outcome: Aaron's care team — prompted by a BAG3 navigator or his own research — initiates a referral to an academic center with cardiogenetics and gene therapy expertise. His eligibility for active trials is assessed in the context of his antiarrhythmic regimen and PBC. If eligible, a structured pre-enrollment process begins. If not yet eligible, a watch-and-wait plan with clear re-evaluation triggers is established. Aaron transitions from passive management to active trajectory.
paths.key_driver: Proactive surfacing of gene therapy trial options; specialist evaluation of eligibility given dual-diagnosis complexity; structured navigation across cardiology, hepatology, and research tracks.

paths.label: Continued Suppressive Management — Slow Deterioration
paths.outcome: Aaron continues on amiodarone and mexiletine. VT episodes remain frequent enough to be a constant presence. Cumulative amiodarone toxicity begins to affect liver function — complicating both his PBC management and his cardiac risk profile. His LVEF declines slowly over years. Gene therapy trial windows narrow as his clinical trajectory becomes more complex. He remains well-managed by the standard of care and subtly out of reach of the options that could have changed his trajectory.
paths.key_driver: Absence of proactive specialty navigation; no systematic mechanism to surface gene therapy options to patients on complex multi-drug, multi-specialty regimens; cumulative amiodarone toxicity creating compounding clinical complexity.

profile.name: Tomáš K.
profile.preference: access Vorasidenib or enroll in IDH1 clinical trial
profile.distance to care: 4–5 hours to Vienna / Prague (nearest specialist centers)
profile.quote: Vorasidenib is approved. I know it exists. My oncologist knows it exists. But in our country it is simply not available, and no one can tell me when that will change.
profile.bio_1: Tomáš was preparing a bid for a large project when he experienced a focal seizure at his desk. He noticed word-finding difficulties and mild headaches for several months prior, which he attributed to work stress. An MRI at his regional hospital in North Macedonia revealed a non-enhancing left frontal mass. He was referred to a neurosurgical center in Skopje, where a gross total resection was performed.
profile.bio_2: Tomáš is an organized, practically oriented professional who responds to uncertainty by seeking information and building plans. He reads medical literature and has joined several international online patient communities where he has encountered accounts of Vorasidenib (Voranigo) access across the EU. His wife manages household logistics and their daughter's school schedule while working part-time. Financially, the family is middle-class — they own their home and have modest savings, but extended out-of-pocket drug costs or frequent international travel would strain them within months. Tomáš is acutely aware that his treatment window — before potential transformation to higher-grade disease — is not infinite.
profile.goal1: Access Vorasidenib as the standard-of-care IDH inhibitor recommended by EU neuro-oncology guidelines, either through named patient programs, cross-border care, or clinical trial enrollment.
profile.goal2: Avoid or substantially delay radiation and chemotherapy, preserving cognitive function for occupational re-entry and active parenting.
profile.goal3: Establish a durable treatment plan before his daughter is old enough to understand the severity of his illness — on his own terms, not dictated by system delays.
profile.barrier1: Vorasidenib has received EMA approval but has not been reimbursed by the North Macedonian national health authority, and no named patient program is available domestically. Out-of-pocket cost is prohibitive at approximately €8,000–€10,000 per month.
profile.barrier2: The nearest centers with active IDH1 trial sites and Vorasidenib access are in Vienna, Prague, and Munich — requiring cross-border travel, time off work, accommodation costs, and separation from his family.
profile.barrier3: His domestic oncology team recommends watchful waiting but has limited familiarity with the IDH inhibitor data and actively discourages cross-border self-referral, creating friction in terry-provider relationship.

journey.narrative_description: Gross total resection is performed at the neurosurgical center in Skopje. The surgery is uneventful and Tomáš retains full motor and language function. When pathology returns two weeks later confirming IDH1-mutant Grade 2 diffuse astrocytoma, his oncologist presents the result as 'relatively good news' — a slow-growing tumor with a favorable molecular profile. However, the oncologist provides no information about Vorasidenib or IDH-targeted therapies, defaulting immediately to a watchful waiting recommendation. Tomáš leaves the consultation with a diagnosis but no treatment plan and begins researching independently that evening.
journey.quote: They said 'Grade 2, IDH-mutant, favorable prognosis.' They said we could watch and wait. I went home and read everything I could find. By midnight I knew the drug existed. I knew it was approved in Europe. I knew my hospital didn't have it.

events.label: Gross Total Resection — Full Function Preserved
events.description: Maximal safe resection achieved at Skopje neurosurgical center. Tomáš retains full motor and language function post-operatively. Intraoperative neuromonitoring used. He is discharged after four days.
events.tooltip: GTR with full functional preservation — a genuinely positive surgical outcome that establishes initial trust in the Skopje team.

events.label: Oncologist Recommends Watch-and-Wait Without Mentioning Vorasidenib
events.description: At the post-pathology consultation, the oncologist recommends observation with 3-monthly MRI. No mention is made of Vorasidenib, IDH inhibitor therapy, or clinical trial options. Tomáš discovers the existence of Vorasidenib through his own research the same evening.
events.tooltip: Watch-and-wait recommended without any discussion of IDH inhibitor options — Tomáš self-discovers Vorasidenib the night of his pathology disclosure.

sentiment_reasons: Successful surgery with preserved function is a meaningful early positive — but the oncologist's silence on IDH-targeted therapy immediately unsettles Tomáš
Self-discovery of an approved, targeted drug his team never mentioned creates a deep and lasting sense of information inequality
The gap between 'favorable prognosis' framing and the reality of an untreated IDH-mutant tumor begins to widen in his mind immediately

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The surgical success briefly lifts the emotional baseline, but the omission of IDH inhibitor options in the same consultation deflates it — leaving a net slightly negative state.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Initial trust in the surgical team is solid; the oncology consultation introduces the first significant trust gap through information omission.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Tomáš's practical intelligence activates immediately — he begins structured research within hours of leaving the clinic.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Post-operative recovery is manageable; logistical demands are not yet a dominant concern at this stage.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš confirms through his oncologist and a pharmacy inquiry that Vorasidenib is not reimbursed in North Macedonia and carries an estimated out-of-pocket cost of €8,000–€10,000 per month. The drug is EMA-approved. It is available in Germany, Austria, and France. His country has not initiated a reimbursement review timeline. He contacts the local patient association for rare cancers and is told there is no named patient program and no compassionate use pathway currently active for vorasidenib. His wife sits with him at the kitchen table while their daughter sleeps and they calculate how many months their savings could cover.
journey.quote: The drug is approved. In Germany they can get it. In France they can get it. We are three hours from Vienna. But for us it does not exist. This is the part that made me genuinely angry.

events.label: Vorasidenib Not Reimbursed — No Named Patient Pathway
events.description: Pharmacist and oncologist confirm Vorasidenib is unobtainable domestically. EMA approval does not translate to national reimbursement. Terry association confirms no compassionate use or named patient program is active in North Macedonia.
events.tooltip: EMA-approved drug unavailable domestically. No reimbursement review timeline. No named patient program. Out-of-pocket cost approximately €8,000–€10,000/month.

events.label: Out-of-Pocket Cost Calculation — Savings Runway Is Limited
events.description: Tomáš and his wife calculate that their household savings could sustain 3–4 months of out-of-pocket Vorasidenib at best. His medical leave has reduced household income by approximately 40%. The financial ceiling makes self-pay a bridge, not a solution.
events.tooltip: Household savings could sustain 3–4 months of self-pay at most. Medical leave has reduced income by ~40%. Out-of-pocket is not a durable solution.

events.label: Country-Specific Access Status Tool — EMA Approval vs. National Reimbursement Map
events.description: A sponsor-maintained, publicly accessible digital tool showing real-time vorasidenib reimbursement status by EU member state — including named patient program availability, estimated processing timelines, and links to country-specific application forms — would intercept Tomáš at the exact moment he discovers the access gap. Rather than ending at 'not available,' the tool would immediately surface his next available option: named patient application, cross-border self-referral, or active trial sites within travel range.
events.tooltip: real-time EU access status map by country — prevents the 'drug exists but is unavailable' dead end by surfacing named patient and trial pathways immediately.

events.label: Financial Bridge Program — 90-Day Self-Pay Coverage While Named Patient Processes
events.description: A sponsor-funded financial bridge program providing vorasidenib at no cost for 60–90 days while a named patient application or trial screening is processed would eliminate the kitchen-table savings calculation as a rate-limiting step. Tomáš's family savings runway of 3–4 months is the primary structural reason self-pay is a bridge rather than a solution. Removing that ceiling during the access processing window transforms a crisis timeline into a manageable one.
events.tooltip: 60–90 day cost-free drug bridge during named patient or trial screening processing — eliminates the savings depletion crisis that forces premature, distressed decisions.

events.label: No Central Resource Explaining Named Patient Process for North Macedonia
events.description: Tomáš cannot find any authoritative, patient-facing resource explaining how a North Macedonian patient would initiate a named patient or compassionate use application for vorasidenib — not from the EMA, the manufacturer, the domestic health authority, or patient advocacy organizations. The gap between EMA approval and domestic access is visible; the pathway to close it is not. This information vacuum is the single largest driver of the path_neg risk at this step.
events.tooltip: Missing resource: no patient-facing guide to named patient application process for non-reimbursing EU countries — EMA approval visible but access pathway invisible.

sentiment_reasons: Discovering that an approved, standard-of-care drug is simply unavailable in his country produces profound anger and a sense of systemic injustice
Calculating the financial runway at the kitchen table with his wife makes the stakes viscerally concrete
The absence of any pathway — named patient, compassionate use, or reimbursement review — removes all domestic options simultaneously

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: This step contains the sharpest single emotional drop in the journey — the transition from 'there is a drug' to 'you cannot have it' is the defining injustice of Tomáš's experience.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The oncologist's failure to proactively raise the reimbursement gap — or offer any pathway guidance — significantly erodes trust in the domestic system.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Tomáš's practical research capability keeps him from feeling entirely passive, but the structural barriers he is encountering are beyond individual agency.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Financial constraint begins to compress logistical options — cross-border access is theoretically possible but cost-prohibitive without additional support.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš finds an international IDH-mutant glioma patient community on Facebook and a dedicated Reddit subforum. He reads accounts from patients in Georgia and North Macedonia who have traveled to Germany or Austria for second opinions and treatment, and others describing Vorasidenib access through named patient programs in countries where domestic reimbursement was pending. He encounters a Swedish patient who has been on Vorasidenib since September 2024 with 'spectacular' results. He posts his situation and within 48 hours has detailed, practical responses from four patients in similar geographic access situations. He also learns that several centers in Vienna, Munich, and Prague are active EORTC neuro-oncology trial sites.
journey.quote: I posted at midnight. By morning there were four replies from people in almost exactly my situation. One of them had driven from Serbia to Vienna for their consultation. He told me which doctor to ask for. That information did not come from any hospital.

events.label: Finds IDH-Mutant Glioma Patient Community — International Peers
events.description: Tomáš joins an international Facebook group and Reddit community for IDH-mutant glioma patients. He reads accounts from patients in Serbia, Georgia, and North Macedonia who have navigated cross-border access. He identifies a pattern: patients from non-reimbursing countries traveling to Vienna or Munich for second opinions and trial access.
events.tooltip: International patient community reveals a pattern: non-reimbursing country patients traveling to Vienna and Munich for second opinions and trial access — exactly Tomáš's situation.

events.label: Peer Identifies Specific Vienna Neuro-Oncologist with IDH Trial Affiliation
events.description: A patient from Serbia who traveled to the Medical University of Vienna provides the name of a neuro-oncologist who leads an active IDH-targeted EORTC trial. He provides the direct email for the clinical trials coordinator and confirms the center accepts international self-referrals.
events.tooltip: Peer from Serbia provides named neuro-oncologist at Vienna MedUni — EORTC IDH trial affiliation confirmed. Direct coordinator email provided. Center accepts international self-referrals.

sentiment_reasons: Finding peers in genuinely comparable geographic situations — not theoretical ones — produces the first real sense that a path forward exists
The Swedish patient's 'spectacular' Vorasidenib result is the first concrete evidence that the drug works in a real person's life
A named specialist and direct coordinator contact transforms abstract options into actionable next steps

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Community discovery produces the strongest positive shift in the journey so far — grounded in practical information, not reassurance.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust shifts from the domestic care system toward the international patient network and the community-identified specialist center.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Accumulating peer knowledge gives Tomáš the vocabulary and framing to begin a productive conversation with a specialist center.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Knowing the specific person to contact at the Vienna center dramatically reduces the logistical complexity of cross-border access.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš emails the Medical University of Vienna neuro-oncology department directly, attaches his complete pathology report and MRI imaging, and requests a second opinion consultation. He receives an appointment within three weeks. He travels by train with his wife — his daughter stays with her grandparents. The Vienna neuro-oncologist reviews his case in detail, affirms that his molecular profile makes him an ideal candidate for IDH inhibition, confirms that Vorasidenib is available in Austria through national reimbursement, and — critically — informs him of two active clinical trials: an EORTC combination IDH1 + immunotherapy Phase II, and a randomized Phase III comparing vorasidenib against observation in a post-GTR setting with a European site in Prague. The consultation costs €420 out of pocket. Tomáš describes it as the most productive two hours since his diagnosis.
journey.quote: The doctor in Vienna spent ninety minutes with us. She drew diagrams. She printed the trial summaries. She said 'you are exactly the kind of patient these trials were designed for.' I had not heard that before — that I was the right patient for something.

events.label: Vienna Second Opinion — IDH Inhibition Strongly Affirmed
events.description: Vienna neuro-oncologist reviews the case comprehensively and confirms that Tomáš's IDH1-mutant, MGMT-methylated profile makes him an ideal candidate for IDH inhibitor therapy. She provides a written letter of recommendation for Vorasidenib initiation — useful for any subsequent named patient or trial application.
events.tooltip: Vienna specialist strongly affirms IDH inhibition candidacy — written letter of recommendation provided for named patient / trial application use.

events.label: Two Active Trials Identified — EORTC Combination + Phase III Prague Site
events.description: The Vienna oncologist identifies two immediately relevant trials: a Phase II EORTC IDH1 inhibitor + PD-1 immunotherapy combination trial with a Vienna site, and a Phase III randomized trial comparing vorasidenib to active surveillance post-GTR, with a participating center in Prague.
events.tooltip: Two trials identified: EORTC Phase II IDH1+PD-1 at Vienna (combination), and Phase III vorasidenib vs. observation with a Prague site — both within travel range.

events.label: Travel and Accommodation Cost — Vienna Not Sustainable Long-Term
events.description: The Vienna consultation costs €420, plus train travel and one night of accommodation — approximately €700 total. Manageable for a single visit, but a trial requiring monthly visits would cost €600–€800 per month in travel alone, before any drug costs.
events.tooltip: Single Vienna consultation manageable (~€700 all-in). Monthly trial participation would cost €600–€800 in travel alone — a significant recurring burden on a reduced income.

sentiment_reasons: Being told explicitly that his profile is exactly what active trials were designed for is the first moment Tomáš feels like a candidate rather than a problem
Two concrete trial options — within travel range — represent a genuine bifurcation of possible futures
The travel cost reality reasserts itself as a persistent structural constraint even in the most positive consultation of his journey

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The Vienna consultation is the single most positive step in the journey — combining expert validation, concrete options, and the first sense of genuine clinical partnership.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in the Vienna specialist is immediate and high — she invests time, provides written documentation, and treats his case with visible engagement.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: The structured trial summaries the specialist provides give Tomáš a framework to evaluate options analytically — matching his decision-making style.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: The consultation is logistically achievable; the sustainability of repeated travel at trial-cadence cost is the emerging constraint.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš returns from Vienna and presents his second opinion and the trial information to his domestic oncologist. The response is dismissive — the oncologist expresses skepticism about the value of the trials ('too experimental') and reiterates a recommendation for adjuvant radiation and temozolomide. He suggests that Vorasidenib's long-term side effect profile is 'unknown' and that proven chemoradiation is the safer choice. Tomáš has read the INDIGO trial data. He knows the NDA. He is not persuaded — but the conflict with his domestic team introduces practical complications: he needs local coordination for monitoring and any cross-border care requires some domestic infrastructure.
journey.quote: My oncologist at home told me the trials are too experimental. He said radiation is proven. But I have read the INDIGO data. I know the five-year progression curves. He did not mention them. How do you trust someone who is telling you less than you already know?

events.label: Domestic Oncologist Rejects Second Opinion — Pushes Radiation
events.description: The domestic oncologist dismisses the Vienna second opinion and the trial options, characterizing them as 'experimental.' He recommends adjuvant radiation and TMZ chemotherapy and expresses concern that pursuing cross-border options will delay treatment. Tomáš questions the framing directly — the oncologist does not engage with the INDIGO data.
events.tooltip: Domestic oncologist dismisses Vienna second opinion and trial options as 'too experimental' — recommends radiation/TMZ, does not engage with INDIGO trial evidence.

events.label: Local Care Dependency — Cannot Fully Disengage from Domestic System
events.description: Tomáš recognizes that regardless of where he pursues treatment, he needs local support: MRI access, levetiracetam prescriptions, and routine monitoring. His domestic oncologist's resistance creates a practical infrastructure problem for any cross-border or trial pathway.
events.tooltip: Cross-border or trial access requires domestic monitoring support. Oncologist's hostility to self-referral creates friction even for logistical coordination.

sentiment_reasons: A physician who argues against a treatment using less evidence than terry has already read destroys rather than shapes trust
The radiation recommendation is not wrong per se — but presented without any acknowledgment of IDH inhibitor data, it feels like epistemic negligence
Recognizing his dependency on a system that is not supporting him produces a new and specifically frustrating form of anxiety

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The domestic physician conflict is the second-lowest emotional step — it combines dismissal, power asymmetry, and practical dependency into a highly aversive experience.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The domestic oncologist's failure to engage with primary trial evidence drops provider trust to its lowest point in the journey.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Paradoxically, the conflict reinforces Tomáš's conviction that his own research-led judgment is more clinically grounded than the advice he is receiving locally.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: The dependency on domestic infrastructure for monitoring and prescriptions constrains his options — he cannot fully exit the domestic system even if he wants to.

journey.narrative_description: Armed with the Vienna specialist's letter of recommendation and terry community's guidance, Tomáš investigates every non-trial pathway to Vorasidenib. He contacts Servier (the manufacturer) directly through their medical information line to ask about expanded access programs. He is informed that a formal expanded access program is not currently active for vorasidenib in his geography, but that named patient applications can be considered on a country-by-country basis and require a local physician to sponsor the request. His domestic oncologist declines to sponsor. His community peers suggest approaching the Vienna specialist to initiate a cross-border named patient application — a process neither party has experience navigating.
journey.quote: I called the company. I was polite. I asked about expanded access. They said it requires a physician sponsor in my country. My physician in my country said no. The circle was complete.

events.label: Expanded Access Application Requires Domestic Physician Sponsor — Refused
events.description: Named patient application for vorasidenib requires a sponsoring physician in terry's country of residence. Tomáš's domestic oncologist declines to participate, citing unfamiliarity with the application process and personal reservations about IDH inhibitor therapy.
events.tooltip: Named patient application requires domestic physician sponsor. Domestic oncologist declines — citing unfamiliarity and personal reservations. Circle closes.

events.label: Vienna Specialist Agrees to Explore Cross-Border Named Patient Pathway
events.description: The Vienna neuro-oncologist agrees to investigate whether a cross-border named patient application can be initiated from Austria on Tomáš's behalf. This is non-standard and requires coordination between Austrian regulatory authorities and the Macedonian health ministry. No timeline is available.
events.tooltip: Vienna specialist agrees to explore cross-border named patient application — non-standard, requires Austria-North Macedonia regulatory coordination. Timeline unknown.

events.label: International Specialist Letter as Named Patient Sponsor — Bypass Domestic Refusal
events.description: A formal sponsor policy — and supporting regulatory guidance — establishing that an international specialist letter (e.g., from the Vienna neuro-oncologist) can substitute for a domestic physician sponsor signature on named patient applications in non-EU member states would eliminate the blockage Tomáš hits when his domestic oncologist refuses. The Vienna specialist is willing and qualified; the barrier is procedural, not clinical. A pre-negotiated pathway with MALMED (North Macedonia's medicines authority) would make this substitution actionable.
events.tooltip: pre-negotiated named patient pathway accepting international specialist letters in lieu of domestic physician sponsors — eliminates the single point of failure in Tomáš's access route.
events.partner: Medical University of Vienna / MALMED (North Macedonian Medicines Agency)

events.label: HCP Education Program — IDH Inhibitor Data for Domestic Oncologists in Non-Reimbursing Countries
events.description: A targeted medical education program for oncologists in non-reimbursing EU and Western Balkans countries — covering IDH1 inhibitor mechanism, the INDIGO trial data, current EANO guidelines, and how to support cross-border patient referrals — would address the root cause of Tomáš's domestic physician barrier. His oncologist's refusal to co-sign the named patient application is driven by unfamiliarity with the data, not clinical disagreement. Education converts a blocker into a facilitator.
events.tooltip: HCP education program in non-reimbursing Western Balkans markets — converts unfamiliar domestic oncologists from access blockers into referral facilitators.

events.label: No Patient Navigator or Dedicated Access Contact at Sponsor or Trial Site
events.description: At the point of named patient refusal — the most logistically complex and emotionally draining moment in the access journey — Tomáš has no single human contact he can reach out to for navigation support. The Vienna specialist is helpful but not resourced for cross-border regulatory administration. The trial site coordinator handles enrolled participants, not pre-screening access cases. The domestic oncologist has refused to engage. There is no patient navigator, access coordinator, or sponsor helpline that can hold his case and move it forward.
events.tooltip: Missing resource: no dedicated access navigator or case manager for patients in non-reimbursing countries — the most critical gap at the highest-complexity moment in the journey.

sentiment_reasons: The physician sponsor refusal closes every domestic access pathway simultaneously — it is the most complete institutional blockage in the journey
The Vienna specialist's willingness to explore a non-standard workaround sustains hope but introduces a new layer of bureaucratic uncertainty
Tomáš is now managing three parallel tracks — trial, named patient, and self-pay bridge — simultaneously, with no certainty on any of them

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The named patient rejection and the slow-moving Vienna workaround produce a flat, grinding neutral state — neither the sharp pain of the worst steps nor the lift of genuine progress.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The Vienna specialist's active advocacy partially compensates for domestic system failures; trust is now strongly bifurcated between local and international care.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Tomáš is operating at the edge of what patient-level agency can achieve — he has done everything available to him and is now dependent on institutional action.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Managing three parallel pathways (trial, named patient, self-pay bridge) while on medical leave with a reduced income is beginning to create emotional and logistical overload.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš focuses his analysis on the two trials the Vienna specialist identified. The Prague Phase III (vorasidenib vs. observation) is closer — a 6-hour drive versus a 9-hour train journey to Vienna. It offers guaranteed vorasidenib access in the active arm, but carries a 50% placebo probability. The Vienna Phase II (IDH1 inhibitor + PD-1 immunotherapy combination) has no placebo arm but is an unproven combination with unknown additive side effects. He creates a comparison document. His wife reads it and asks one question: 'Which one gets you the drug for sure?' He does not have a clean answer. He also connects with a patient in the Prague trial via the online community who shares their visit schedule — approximately one visit every 6 weeks — and confirms the center reimburses reasonable travel costs for trial participants.
journey.quote: My wife asked which trial guarantees the drug. I had to tell her that one has a 50/50 chance of placebo, and the other is a combination that's never been tested. Neither is the simple answer. But both are better than what I have now, which is nothing.

events.label: Peer in Prague Trial Confirms Visit Cadence and Travel Reimbursement
events.description: A community peer currently enrolled in the Prague Phase III trial confirms visits are required every 6 weeks, the site reimburses documented travel up to €200 per visit, and the site coordinator is responsive and English-speaking. This substantially reduces the logistical uncertainty of the Prague option.
events.tooltip: Prague trial peer confirms: 6-week visit cadence, up to €200 travel reimbursement per visit, English-speaking coordinator. Logistical uncertainty substantially reduced.

events.label: 50% Placebo Probability in Phase III — Wife's Core Concern
events.description: The Prague Phase III is a randomized, placebo-controlled trial. Tomáš has a 50% probability of receiving placebo. His wife's reaction to this is visceral — she asks whether they could know if he receives placebo and whether he could withdraw to access the drug another way. He does not yet have those answers.
events.tooltip: 50% placebo probability in Prague Phase III. Wife's central concern: could they know if he receives placebo? Could he withdraw? Answers not yet available.

events.label: Vienna Phase II — Combination Arm Carries Unknown Additive Risk
events.description: The Vienna Phase II adds PD-1 immunotherapy to IDH inhibition — a mechanistically promising but clinically unvalidated combination in glioma. Tomáš researches checkpoint inhibitor toxicity in brain tumors and finds mixed data. The absence of a placebo arm is appealing, but the additive side effect profile is genuinely unknown.
events.tooltip: Vienna Phase II IDH1+PD-1 combination: no placebo arm (positive), but additive toxicity profile unvalidated in glioma context. Mixed literature on checkpoint inhibitors in brain tumors.

sentiment_reasons: The peer contact in the Prague trial makes that option feel real and navigable for the first time
The placebo randomization is not an abstract statistical concern — for a 40-year-old with a six-year-old daughter, it is a concrete fear of spending months unprotected
Facing two imperfect options simultaneously — when the 'right' answer would be unrestricted vorasidenib access — generates a specific kind of frustrated optimism

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Genuine options produce genuine hope — but the imperfections of both options prevent a clean positive shift. The emotional register is engaged uncertainty rather than dread.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The Prague trial peer and the Vienna specialist's continued involvement sustain trust in the research pathway; the domestic system has effectively ceased to be a trust resource.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Tomáš's comparison document and his ability to articulate the mechanistic distinction between the two trials represent the peak of his self-directed health literacy.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Prague's travel reimbursement program meaningfully improves the financial sustainability of trial participation; the 6-week visit cadence is manageable alongside partial work return.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš faces three possible paths: Enroll in the Phase III; pursue the Vienna Phase II combination; or continue waiting for the named patient pathway to open, bridging with 2–3 months of self-pay vorasidenib if the family depletes part of their savings.
journey.narrative_description_detail: Three converging pressures bring Tomáš to a decision point. His latest MRI — at 7 months post-resection — shows stable disease but his Vienna specialist notes a small area of T2 signal change that she describes as 'worth watching.' His domestic oncologist reiterates that radiation should begin within the next 3 months if they are not proceeding with IDH inhibition. And his daughter, who has been told only that her father has 'something wrong with his head that the doctors are fixing,' asks him one morning whether he will be better by her birthday. He has three paths in front of him: enroll in the Prague Phase III (guaranteed drug access — 50% of the time); pursue the Vienna Phase II combination (no placebo, but unknown additive risk); or continue waiting for the named patient pathway to open, bridging with 2–3 months of self-pay vorasidenib if the family depletes part of their savings. He and his wife schedule an evening to decide.

inflection_detail.label: Three-Way Decision Inflection — Enroll, Wait, or Self-Pay Bridge
inflection_detail.description: Tomáš reaches a convergence of clinical urgency, financial constraint, and family emotional weight that forces a decision without a clear 'right' answer. Each option carries a distinct combination of clinical, financial, and logistical risk. The absence of straightforward domestic vorasidenib access is the structural failure driving this decision — the complexity of his choice is not inherent to his disease state but is an artifact of reimbursement geography. This is precisely the inflection point where sponsor-supported patient navigation and access programs have the highest potential impact on enrollment quality and patient wellbeing.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Establish a cross-border patient navigation program for IDH1-mutant glioma patients in non-reimbursing EU countries — providing named patient application support, trial site matching, financial bridge assistance for self-pay periods, and a dedicated international access coordinator who can engage both terry and the domestic physician. A structured 'access bridge' — covering 2–3 months of vorasidenib at no cost while trial screening or named patient processing proceeds — would materially reduce dropout at this inflection point.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Proportion of IDH1-mutant glioma patients in non-reimbursing EU countries who ultimately access vorasidenib within 12 months of eligibility confirmation; rate of trial enrollment versus watchful waiting in patients who cannot access vorasidenib domestically; average named patient processing time by EU member state; financial distress rate among trial-eligible patients who do not enroll.

paths.label: Prague Phase III Enrollment — Structured Access with Accepted Risk
paths.outcome: Tomáš and his wife decide that the 50% probability of active treatment in a well-run EORTC trial — with travel reimbursement, English-speaking coordinators, and a 6-week visit cadence — is the most sustainable path. They accept the placebo risk as the price of access and commit to Prague. If randomized to placebo, they plan to withdraw at the first MRI and pursue the Vienna Phase II or self-pay bridge. The decision is made with eyes open to its imperfection — and the family frames it as the most active thing they can do together.
paths.key_driver: Prague peer's logistical reassurance; Vienna specialist's written recommendation supporting trial as first choice; family agreement that structured access is preferable to indefinite waiting; site's travel reimbursement reducing financial pressure.

paths.label: Paralysis and Delayed Enrollment — Window Narrowing
paths.outcome: Unable to resolve the placebo concern and without a named patient pathway open, Tomáš and his wife defer the decision. They begin self-pay vorasidenib for one month while waiting — depleting savings at a rate that cannot be sustained. Three months later, the financial runway is exhausted, the named patient pathway has not progressed, and the domestic oncologist's radiation recommendation has become more urgent as a new MRI shows borderline T2 change. Tomáš eventually enrolls in Prague under significantly more emotional and financial duress than would have been necessary three months earlier.
